name: Aardonyx
hints: Early Jurassic Elliot Formation of South Africa
Intermediate arm features between two-legged ancestors and four-legged giants
Approximately 3 meters long, herbivorous, habitually bipedal but capable of quadrupedal movement
South Africa, Early Jurassic period
A transitional dinosaur showing how early long-necked dinosaurs began evolving toward walking on all fours

name: Abdarainurus
hints: Fossils were discovered in 1970 in the Abdarant Nuru locality of the Alagteeg Formation in Mongolia.
It is known only from eight front tail vertebrae, one middle tail vertebra, and several chevrons.
It was a large, plant-eating titanosaur sauropod.
It lived in Asia during the Late Cretaceous period.
It is a sauropod dinosaur with a long neck and tail.

name: Abditosaurus
hints: Fossils were discovered in the Conques Formation of Catalonia, Spain, dating to the Maastrichtian age of the Late Cretaceous.
It is a titanosaurian sauropod that possessed an ossified sternal rib and an ossified calcaneum, features rarely identified in other members of its group.
This dinosaur reached an estimated length of 17.5 meters and weighed approximately 14 tonnes, making it the largest titanosaur known from its specific environment.
It lived on the Ibero-Armorican Island, a prehistoric landmass comprising modern-day Spain, Portugal, and southern France, during the Late Cretaceous period.
It is recognized as the most complete titanosaur skeleton ever found in Europe, featuring a semi-articulated neck with twelve preserved cervical vertebrae.

name: Abelisaurus
hints: Fossils were discovered in the Anacleto Formation of the Neuquén Group in Río Negro Province, Argentina, dating to the early Campanian stage of the Late Cretaceous, approximately 83 to 80 million years ago.

It possessed a relatively deep skull with very large fenestrae (openings) to reduce weight, prominent rough ridges above the eyes that likely supported keratinous crests, and a lack of bony horns or bony crests.
This bipedal carnivore is estimated to have reached lengths between 7.4 and 11 meters and weighed between 1.65 and 3 tonnes.
It lived in South America during the Late Cretaceous period.
It is one of the most iconic members of the Abelisauridae family, often compared to Tyrannosaurus as an apex predator, but distinguished by its short, deep skull and tiny, vestigial arms.

name: Abrictosaurus
hints: Fossils were found in the Upper Elliot Formation, dating to the Hettangian and Sinemurian stages of the Early Jurassic, approximately 200 to 190 million years ago.
It possessed distinctive heterodont dentition, featuring large, canine-like tusks in both the upper and lower jaws, along with a toothless beak at the front.
This small dinosaur was approximately 1.2 meters (3.9 ft) long and weighed less than 45 kilograms (99 lb), feeding on plants or omnivorously.
It lived during the Early Jurassic period in what is now southern Africa, specifically in regions that are now Lesotho and South Africa.
It is one of the earliest known members of the Heterodontosauridae family, a group of small, bipedal ornithischians famous for their mismatched teeth.

name: Abrosaurus
hints: Fossils were recovered from the Lower Shaximiao Formation in the Dashanpu Quarry, Sichuan Province, dating to the Middle Jurassic (Bathonian to Callovian stages, approximately 168 to 161 million years ago).
It possessed a distinctive boxy skull topped with a tall, bony arch that housed the nostrils, featuring large openings separated by thin, delicate bony struts.
This quadrupedal herbivore was relatively small for a sauropod, measuring no more than 9.1 metres (30 ft) in length.
It lived in Asia during the Middle Jurassic period.
Its name translates to 'delicate lizard,' referring to the fragile, sieve-like structure of its skull bones.

name: Abydosaurus
hints: Fossils were discovered in the Mussentuchit Member of the Cedar Mountain Formation in northeastern Utah, dating to the Albian stage of the Early Cretaceous, approximately 104 million years ago.
It possesses unusually narrow and sharp teeth, distinguishing it from other brachiosaurids that typically had broader, spoon-shaped teeth, along with a relatively small external nostril opening.
This sauropod reached an estimated length of about 18.3 meters (60 feet) and was a herbivore that fed on high-growing vegetation.
It lived during the Early Cretaceous period in what is now North America, specifically in the region of present-day Utah.
It is famous for being one of the very few sauropods known from a nearly complete skull, making it a rare and significant find for understanding Cretaceous dinosaur anatomy.

name: Acantholipan
hints: Fossils were discovered in the Pen Formation in Coahuila, Mexico, dating to the Santonian stage of the Late Cretaceous, approximately 86 to 83.6 million years ago.
This dinosaur possessed distinctive spike-like osteoderms, specifically a prominent spine-like armor plate found on its posterior thorax.
It was a medium-sized herbivore that walked on four legs and belonged to the Nodosauridae family, which lacked the tail club seen in its relatives.
It lived in what is now North America during the Late Cretaceous period.
It is covered in bony armor plates and spikes, resembling a living tank or an armadillo with extra spikes.

name: Achelousaurus
hints: Fossils were recovered from the Two Medicine Formation in Montana, dating to approximately 77 to 74.8 million years ago.
It features a bony neck frill with a pair of long, outward-curving spikes and distinctive rough, keratin-covered bosses above the eyes and on the snout instead of traditional horns.
This large, four-legged herbivore measured about 6 meters (20 feet) in length and weighed roughly 3 tons.
It lived during the Late Cretaceous period on the island continent of Laramidia, which corresponds to modern-day North America.
It is a horned dinosaur with a large head, a hooked beak, and a prominent bony frill, resembling a relative of Triceratops but with a different arrangement of facial protuberances.

name: Acheroraptor
hints: Fossils were recovered from the Hell Creek Formation in Montana, dating to approximately 66 million years ago.
It possessed a slender, curved maxilla with serrated teeth, characteristic of a slashing predator.
This small theropod likely measured around 1.5 meters in length and hunted smaller prey with rapid, weak bites.
It lived in North America during the very end of the Cretaceous period, just before the mass extinction event.
It is a close relative of Velociraptor and one of the last surviving species of its kind before the dinosaurs went extinct.

name: Achillesaurus
hints: Santonian-aged Bajo de la Carpa Formation, Rio Negro, Argentina
Partial skeleton including a sacral vertebra, tail vertebrae, thighbone, shin, foot, and ilium
Relatively large basal alvarezsaurid theropod
Late Cretaceous period, South America (Patagonia)
Named after Achilles' heel because its diagnostic fossils were found in the ankle/foot area

name: Achillobator
hints: Fossils were discovered in the Bayan Shireh Formation of Mongolia, dating to the Late Cretaceous period approximately 96 to 89 million years ago.
It possessed a robust, deep-bodied skeleton with a vertically oriented pubis and a primitive pelvis, which is unusual for its group of lightly built relatives.
This large carnivore measured between 4.5 and 5 meters in length and weighed roughly 250 to 350 kilograms, making it one of the largest members of its family.
It lived in what is now Mongolia, on the continent of Asia, during the Late Cretaceous epoch.
It is famous for being a large, feathered predator that hunted using a massive, sickle-shaped claw on its second toe.

name: Acristavus
hints: Fossils were recovered from the Campanian Two Medicine Formation in Montana and the Wahweap Formation in Utah, dating to approximately 79-80 million years ago.
It is a saurolophine hadrosaurid distinguished by the complete lack of any crest or ornamentation on its skull, a rare trait among its relatives.
This herbivorous dinosaur was a medium-sized hadrosaur, closely related to Maiasaura and Brachylophosaurus within the clade Brachylophosaurini.
It lived during the Late Cretaceous period in North America, specifically in the region known as Laramidia, which was separated from the rest of the continent by the Western Interior Seaway.
It is one of the few duck-billed dinosaurs known to have possessed a completely smooth, unadorned skull without any horns, crests, or bony ridges.

name: Acrocanthosaurus
hints: Fossils have been found in the Antlers Formation, Cloverly Formation, and Arundel Formation, dating to approximately 125 to 99.6 million years ago.
It possessed exceptionally tall neural spines on its vertebrae that likely supported a prominent ridge of muscle along its neck, back, and hips.
This bipedal predator measured between 11 and 12.2 meters long, weighed up to 9 metric tons, and hunted large prey such as sauropods and ankylosaurs.
It lived during the Early to Late Cretaceous period on the continent of North America, with remains discovered in states ranging from Oklahoma and Texas to Maryland.
It is widely recognized for the distinctive row of high spines running down its back, which resembles the sail of a later dinosaur but was made of muscle rather than skin.

name: Acrotholus
hints: Fossils were discovered in the Deadhorse Coulee Member of the Milk River Formation in Southern Alberta, dating to the Santonian stage of the Late Cretaceous, approximately 83 to 86 million years ago.
It possesses a distinctive, thick, oval-shaped cranial dome that is up to 55 millimeters thick and lacks the surface tubercles or tesserae found on many other related species.
This dinosaur was a small-bodied, bipedal herbivore, weighing approximately 40 kilograms or less, which is unusually light for a dinosaur of its time period.
It lived in what is now North America during the Late Cretaceous period, making it one of the earliest known members of its family to possess a fully developed dome.
Its most recognizable feature is the solid, bony, dome-shaped skull, which is often associated with head-butting behavior used in intra-species combat.

name: Adamantisaurus
hints: Fossils were discovered in the Adamantina Formation of Brazil, dating to the Late Cretaceous period between 93 and 70 million years ago.
It is known exclusively from six tail vertebrae and two chevrons, lacking any other skeletal material.
Estimates for its size range from 13 to 18 meters in length and 5 to 14.4 tonnes in weight, classifying it as a medium-sized titanosaur.
This herbivorous sauropod lived in South America during the Late Cretaceous period.
It is a massive, long-necked dinosaur with a long tail, typical of the titanosaur group.

name: Adasaurus
hints: Fossils were unearthed from the Nemegt Formation in Mongolia, dating to the Late Cretaceous period approximately 70 million years ago.
Unlike most of its relatives, this dinosaur possessed a notably small and blunt sickle claw on its foot rather than a large, sharp one.
It was a medium-sized carnivore, measuring between 2.4 and 3.5 meters in length and weighing up to 87 kilograms.
This theropod lived in Asia during the Late Cretaceous period.
It is a feathered, bird-like predator that belongs to the same family as Velociraptor, often recognized by its agile build and sharp teeth.

name: Adelolophus
hints: Fossils were found in the Upper Member of the Wahweap Formation in Kane County, Utah, dating to the early Campanian age.
It is a lambeosaurine hadrosaur with a maxilla featuring a uniquely tall medial wall and a rugose, protruding toothrow edge.
It was a herbivorous duck-billed dinosaur, likely similar in size and tube-crest structure to Parasaurolophus.
It lived in North America during the Late Cretaceous period in a coastal estuarine environment.
It is known as the 'unknown crest' dinosaur because its only known fossil is a jawbone that lacks the large hollow head crest typical of its relatives.

name: Adeopapposaurus
hints: Fossils were discovered in the Cañón del Colorado Formation of San Juan, Argentina, dating to the Early Jurassic period.
It possessed a long neck and keratinous beaks at the tips of its upper and lower jaws.
This herbivorous dinosaur was approximately 3 to 4 meters long and weighed around 100 kilograms.
It lived in South America during the Early Jurassic epoch.
It is a massospondylid dinosaur, closely resembling the more famous Massospondylus with its long neck and small head.

name: Adratiklit
hints: Fossils were discovered in the El Mers III Formation in north Morocco, dating to the Bathonian age of the Middle Jurassic, approximately 168 million years ago.
It is a stegosaurian dinosaur, characterized by a row of bony plates along its back and a spiked tail, though only vertebrae and an arm bone have been found so far.
It had an estimated body length of up to 7 meters (23 feet) and was a herbivore.
It lived on the supercontinent Gondwana, specifically in what is now North Africa, during the Middle Jurassic period.
It is one of the oldest known stegosaurs and represents the first described eurypodan dinosaur from North Africa.

name: Adynomosaurus
hints: Fossils were discovered in the Conques Formation, dating to the Maastrichtian stage of the Late Cretaceous, specifically at the Costa de les Solanes locality in Catalonia, Spain.
It is distinguished by a uniquely underdeveloped and narrow shoulder blade (scapula) that is proportionally the shortest of all known hadrosaurs, indicating weaker arm musculature.
As a member of the duck-billed dinosaur family, it was a large herbivore capable of walking on both two and four legs, featuring a long skull with a beak and complex dental batteries.
This dinosaur lived during the Late Cretaceous period in what is now Europe, specifically within a coastal estuary ecosystem in the region of modern-day Spain.
It is a type of crested duck-billed dinosaur (lambeosaurine) known from very scant and incomplete fossil remains, making it one of the rarest hadrosaurs found in Europe.

name: Aegyptosaurus
hints: Fossils were discovered in the Bahariya Formation of Egypt, dating to approximately 95 million years ago during the Cenomanian stage.
It was a massive sauropod with a long neck and tail, characterized by distinctively shaped caudal vertebrae.
Paleontologists estimate it reached lengths of about 15 meters and weighed roughly 7 tonnes, feeding on vegetation.
This dinosaur roamed the southern shores of the Tethys Sea in what is now Africa during the Late Cretaceous period.
Its name translates to 'Egypt's lizard,' reflecting the country where its remains were first found.

name: Aeolosaurus
hints: Fossils have been recovered from the Angostura Colorada, Allen, Lago Colhué Huapí, and Los Alamitos Formations in Argentina, as well as the Serra da Galga Formation in Brazil, dating to the Campanian and Maastrichtian stages of the Late Cretaceous.

This titanosaur is distinguished by its unique tail vertebrae, which feature down-curved prezygapophyses and chevrons with concave posterodorsal surfaces containing double articular facets, along with anteriorly-inclined neural spines in the front part of the tail.

It was a massive, quadrupedal herbivore measuring approximately 14 to 18 meters in length and weighing between 6 and 15 tonnes, possessing a heavily built frame and bony armor plates (osteoderms) along its flanks.
This dinosaur lived during the Late Cretaceous period on the continent of South America, specifically in the region known as Patagonia.
It is a giant, long-necked sauropod dinosaur that, like many of its relatives, had a tail that curved strongly downward toward the ground.

name: Aepyornithomimus
hints: Fossils were discovered at the Tögrögiin Shiree locality within the Djadochta Formation, dating to approximately 75 million years ago during the Campanian stage.
It possessed a distinctive foot structure with a complete calcaneum and lower tarsal III that closely resembles the anatomy of the extinct elephant bird, Aepyornis.
This ornithomimid was a bipedal carnivore or omnivore, likely reaching lengths of around 3 to 4 meters.
It roamed the arid, semi-desert environments of what is now Mongolia in Asia during the Late Cretaceous period.
Its name literally means 'mimic of the elephant bird' due to the striking similarity between its foot bones and those of the giant, flightless Aepyornis.

name: Aerosteon
hints: Fossils were found in the Anacleto Formation of Argentina, dating to the late Campanian stage of the Late Cretaceous, approximately 75 million years ago.
It possessed a highly unusual bird-like respiratory system with extensive pneumatization, meaning its bones were hollowed out by air sacs.
This theropod was a medium-sized predator, estimated to be between 6 and 10 meters long and weighing up to one metric ton.
It lived in South America during the final era of the dinosaurs, specifically the Late Cretaceous period.
Its name literally translates to 'air bone,' referring to the distinctive hollow structure of its skeleton.

name: Afromimus
hints: Fossils were discovered in 1997 at the Gadoufaoua locality within the Early Cretaceous Elrhaz Formation of the Ténéré Desert in Niger.
The specimen includes distinctive tail vertebrae with 'peanut-shaped' depressions on the centrum that helped stabilize the tail, as well as a tibia featuring an unusual elliptical attachment scar.
It was a medium-sized carnivore estimated to be approximately 4.14 meters (13.6 feet) long, with a tibia length of 40 cm.
This dinosaur lived in Africa during the Early Cretaceous period.
It was originally misidentified as an ornithomimosaur (ostrich mimic) due to its leg structure, but is now classified as an abelisauroid theropod.

name: Afrovenator
hints: Fossils were recovered from the Middle to Late Jurassic Tiourarén Formation in the Agadez region of Niger, dating between 167 and 161 million years ago.
It possesses a distinctive rounded horn on top of the lacrimal bone and features crescent-shaped wrist bones that are very flat.
This theropod was approximately 7 to 8 meters long and weighed around 800 kilograms, functioning as a carnivore.
It lived in Africa during the Jurassic period, representing a rare definitive megalosaurid from the Gondwana supercontinent.
It is recognized as one of the few large predatory dinosaurs discovered in Africa, often compared to Allosaurus in build and hunting style.

name: Agilisaurus
hints: Fossils were recovered from the Lower Shaximiao Formation in Sichuan, China, dating to the Middle Jurassic period approximately 168 to 161 million years ago.
It possessed a lightweight skeleton with long hind legs, where the tibia was slightly longer than the femur, indicating it was an extremely fast bipedal runner.
This small herbivore measured about 1.2 to 1.7 meters in length and weighed around 40 kilograms, feeding on abrasive plants using leaf-shaped teeth with wavy enamel.
It lived during the Middle Jurassic period on the landmass that is now eastern Asia.
Its name translates to 'agile lizard,' referring to its reputation as one of the fastest and most nimble dinosaurs due to its slender build and long legs.

name: Agnosphitys
hints: Fossils were recovered from the Magnesian Conglomerate formation in Avon, England, specifically from a sinkhole fissure fill dating to the Norian or Rhaetian stages of the Late Triassic.
The known remains include a distinctive ilium with a well-defined brevis fossa and a semi-perforate acetabulum, as well as a humerus featuring a subrectangular deltopectoral crest.
It was a small, bipedal dinosauriform, likely weighing only a few kilograms, that lived during the Late Triassic period.
This ancient reptile inhabited what is now Europe, specifically England, during the time of the supercontinent Pangaea.
It is often cited in scientific literature as one of the earliest possible dinosaurs or dinosaur relatives, though its classification remains debated among paleontologists.

name: Agujaceratops
hints: Fossils were recovered from the lower part of the Upper Shale member of the Aguja Formation in Big Bend National Park, Texas, dating to approximately 77 million years ago.
It possessed a short nose horn, long brow horns that curved backwards, and an elongate frill with a strong central notch and numerous low, blunt hornlets along the edges.
This herbivorous dinosaur reached a length of about 4.3 meters and weighed roughly 1.5 metric tons.
It lived during the Late Cretaceous period in what is now North America, specifically western Texas.
It is a large horned dinosaur with a distinctive heart-shaped frill circled by small spikes, similar in appearance to Pentacerops or Chasmosaurus.

name: Agustinia
hints: Fossils were recovered from the Lohan Cura Formation in Neuquén Province, Argentina, dating to approximately 116–108 million years ago.
It was originally thought to possess distinctive armor plates or osteoderms down its back, though later research suggested these were likely fragments of ribs and hip bones.
This sauropod was estimated to be about 15 meters long and was a plant-eater.
It lived in South America during the Early Cretaceous period.
It is often compared to Stegosaurus due to the initial misinterpretation of its back as having large, vertical spikes or plates.

name: Ahshislepelta
hints: Fossils were found in the Hunter Wash Member of the Kirtland Formation in New Mexico, dating to approximately 74.5 million years ago during the Late Cretaceous.
It possessed distinctive armor plates (osteoderms) with a surface texture characterized by uniformly distributed pitted rugosity and sparse neurovascular grooves.
This was a relatively small, herbivorous armored dinosaur, estimated to be larger than Pinacosaurus mephistocephalus but smaller than Talarurus.
It lived in what is now North America during the Late Cretaceous period.
It is a type of ankylosaur, easily recognized by its heavy bony armor and shield-like appearance.

name: Ahshislesaurus
hints: Fossils were recovered from the Hunter Wash Member of the Kirtland Formation, dating to approximately 75 million years ago.
It possessed a notably deep mandible at the front, unlike other hadrosaurids where the jaw typically tapers from top to bottom.
This plant-eating dinosaur measured several meters in length and lacked the long, hollow, tube-like crests seen in relatives like Parasaurolophus.
It lived during the Late Cretaceous period in what is now North America, specifically in the region of present-day New Mexico.
It is a duck-billed dinosaur that had only a small, low bump on its snout instead of a prominent head crest.

name: Ahvaytum
hints: Fossils were found in the lower Popo Agie Formation of west-central Wyoming, dating to the Late Triassic period.
It is classified as a basal sauropodomorph, a group of early dinosaur ancestors related to long-necked giants.
This small dinosaur was approximately 3 feet (0.91 meters) long and likely ate plants.
It lived on the ancient supercontinent of Laurasia, which included what is now North America, Europe, and Asia.
It holds the distinction of being the oldest known named dinosaur from the ancient landmass of Laurasia.

name: Ajkaceratops
hints: Fossils were found in the Csehbánya Formation, dating to the Santonian stage approximately 86 to 84 million years ago.
It is classified as a ceratopsian dinosaur, characterized by a beaked face and likely possessing horns or a frill.
It was a small herbivore, estimated to reach a body length of about 1 meter.
It lived in what is now Europe, specifically Hungary, during the Late Cretaceous period.
It is one of the few known horned dinosaurs that lived on the islands of the western Tethys archipelago.

name: Ajnabia
hints: Fossils were recovered from the Late Maastrichtian phosphate mines at Sidi Chennane in the Ouled Abdoun Basin of Morocco.
It is a lambeosaurine hadrosaur, characterized by a hollow crest structure typical of its subfamily.
It was a small herbivore, estimated to reach a total body length of approximately 3.5 meters.
It lived in Africa during the Late Cretaceous period, making it the first definitive hadrosaur discovered on that continent.
It is notable for being an island-hopping or oceanic dispersal species, as Africa was isolated by water from Europe during its time.

name: Akainacephalus
hints: Fossils were found in the Kaiparowits Formation of southern Utah, dating to approximately 76.26 million years ago during the Late Cretaceous.
It possesses a skull covered in thorn-like bony plates and large, triangular cheek horns that point almost vertically downward.
This armored dinosaur was roughly 5 to 6 meters long, weighed over a ton, and was a herbivore that lived in the ancient landmass of Laramidia.
It roamed what is now North America during the Late Cretaceous period, sharing its habitat with hadrosaurs and tyrannosaurs.
It is easily recognized as a heavily armored 'tank' dinosaur with a bony tail club and a head protected by spikes and thick bone.

name: Alamosaurus
hints: Fossils were recovered from the Ojo Alamo Formation in New Mexico, dating to the latest Maastrichtian age of the Late Cretaceous.
It possessed a body partly covered with bony armor (osteoderms) and had elongated centra in the middle part of its tail.
This gigantic quadrupedal herbivore measured approximately 26 to 30 meters long and weighed between 30 and 80 tonnes.
It lived in what is now southwestern North America during the Late Cretaceous period.
It is recognized as the absolute largest dinosaur known from the fossil record of North America.

name: Alaskacephale
hints: Fossils were discovered in the Prince Creek Formation, dating to approximately 72-71 million years ago, on the North Slope of Alaska near the Colville River.
It is distinguished from other relatives by two divergent rows of polygonal bony nodes along the squamosal bone of its skull.
It was a small, herbivorous dinosaur, measuring about 1.5 meters in length and weighing around 10 kilograms, with a thick skull dome.
It lived in the Arctic region of North America during the Late Cretaceous period, enduring extreme seasonal light and temperature variations.
It is a dome-headed dinosaur, closely related to Pachycephalosaurus, known for its thick, bony skull roof used for head-butting or display.

name: Albalophosaurus
hints: Fossils were discovered in the Kuwajima Formation in Hakusan, Ishikawa Prefecture, Japan, dating to the Early Cretaceous (likely Valanginian–Hauterivian).
The name translates to 'white crest lizard', referencing the snow-covered crest of Mount Hakusan, and the holotype consists of cranial bones and a lower jaw from a juvenile.
It was a small, herbivorous ornithischian dinosaur, likely measuring only a few feet in length.
This dinosaur lived in Japan during the Early Cretaceous period.
It is a basal member of the Marginocephalia group, related to both ceratopsians and pachycephalosaurs, though its exact classification remains debated among paleontologists.

name: Albertaceratops
hints: Fossils were discovered in the Oldman Formation of Alberta, Canada, dating to approximately 77.5 million years ago during the middle Campanian stage.
It possessed unusually long brow horns for a centrosaurine, along with a bony nose ridge and two large outward-projecting hooks on its frill.
This dinosaur reached an estimated length of 5.8 meters and weighed around 3,500 kilograms, feeding on vegetation.
It lived in North America during the Late Cretaceous period.
It is a horned dinosaur with a large bony frill, resembling a triceratops but with a distinct nasal boss and frill hooks.

name: Albertadromeus
hints: Fossils were found in the upper part of the Oldman Formation in Alberta, Canada, dating to approximately 77 to 76 million years ago during the Campanian stage.
It possessed a distinctive ankle structure where the distal fibula was fused to the tibia, and its tibia was notably elongated compared to similar dinosaurs.
This was a very small, bipedal herbivore that likely measured less than two meters in length and fed on ferns and low-lying vegetation.
It lived in what is now North America during the Late Cretaceous period.
Its name literally translates to 'runner from Alberta,' reflecting its specialized anatomy built for speed.

name: Albertavenator
hints: Known exclusively from fragmentary skull bones, specifically a short and broad frontal bone with a unique frontoparietal suture configuration, discovered in the Horsethief Member of the Horseshoe Canyon Formation in Alberta, Canada, dating to approximately 71 million years ago.

A small-bodied theropod dinosaur belonging to the family Troodontidae, characterized by a distinct cranial roof morphology that differentiates it from other closely related species like Troodon and Stenonychosaurus.
This small carnivore likely measured roughly 1 to 2 meters in length and weighed only a few kilograms, feeding on small prey such as insects, lizards, and mammals in a cool, floodplain environment.
It lived in North America during the Late Cretaceous period, sharing its habitat with large tyrannosaurids, hadrosaurs, and ceratopsians.
It is a small, bird-like dinosaur with a large brain and keen senses, often depicted as a clever, feathered predator from the age of dinosaurs.

name: Albertonykus
hints: Fossils were found in the Horseshoe Canyon Formation of Alberta, Canada, dating to approximately 68.5 million years ago.
It possessed unusually short, powerful forelimbs ending in a single large, specialized thumb claw adapted for digging.
This small theropod measured about 1.1 meters (3.6 feet) in length and weighed roughly 5 kilograms (11 pounds).
It lived in North America during the Late Cretaceous period.
It is famous for its bizarre, oversized single claw on each hand, which it likely used to tear open rotten logs to eat termites.

name: Albertosaurus
hints: Fossils have been recovered from the Horseshoe Canyon Formation in Alberta, dating to approximately 71 million years ago.
It possessed a massive skull with dozens of large, sharp, serrated teeth and a bone-crushing bite force, along with short, two-fingered forelimbs.
This bipedal predator grew up to 9 meters (30 feet) long and weighed between 1.7 and 3.0 metric tons, feeding on other dinosaurs.
It lived in northwestern North America during the Late Cretaceous period.
It is a large, bipedal carnivore with a huge head and tiny arms, closely related to Tyrannosaurus rex.

name: Albinykus
hints: Fossils were discovered in the Santonian-aged Javkhlant Formation within the Gobi Desert of Mongolia.
It possessed a unique skeletal trait where the ankle bones (tarsals) were completely fused to the lower leg and foot bones, a feature not seen in other relatives.
Weighing under 1 kilogram (2.2 lb), it was one of the smallest non-avian dinosaurs known to science.
It lived during the Late Cretaceous period on the continent of Asia.
It is famously known for its holotype skeleton being preserved in a sitting or crouching position with its feet tucked underneath its body, resembling a modern bird.

name: Alcovasaurus
hints: Found in the Morrison Formation of Natrona County, Wyoming, dating to the Late Jurassic period
Possesses extremely long, slender dermal tail spikes that can reach nearly one meter in length
A large herbivorous stegosaurian measuring approximately 6.5 meters long and weighing up to 5 metric tons
Lived in North America during the Late Jurassic epoch
Known for its distinctive, unusually long tail spikes compared to other stegosaurs

name: Alectrosaurus
hints: Fossils were discovered in the Iren Dabasu Formation in Inner Mongolia, dating to approximately 96 million years ago.
It possessed a unique hindlimb structure where the femur and tibia were nearly equal in length, unlike most other tyrannosauroids.
This medium-sized carnivore measured between 5 and 6 meters in length and weighed roughly 450 to 900 kilograms.
It lived in Asia during the Late Cretaceous period.
It is often recognized as a slender, fast-running early relative of the massive Tyrannosaurus rex.

name: Aletopelta
hints: Fossils were found in the Point Loma Formation of Southern California, dating to the upper Campanian stage approximately 75.5 million years ago.
It possessed a distinctive pelvic shield made of co-ossified hexagonal osteoderms that formed a semi-continuous sheet, along with hollow, cap-like armor plates across its back.
This herbivore measured about 5 meters (16 feet) in length and weighed roughly 2 tonnes.
It lived in what is now North America during the Late Cretaceous period.
It is a heavily armored dinosaur known for its bony plates and spikes, resembling a living tank.

name: Alioramus
hints: Fossils were recovered from the Nemegt Formation in Mongolia, dating to the Late Cretaceous period approximately 70 million years ago.
It possessed a unique row of five irregular bony crests along the top of its snout and had more teeth (76-78) than any other known tyrannosaurid.
This carnivore was relatively small for its family, estimated at 5 to 6 meters long and weighing around 500 kilograms, though most known specimens are juveniles.
It lived in Asia during the Late Cretaceous period, sharing its habitat with other large predators like Tarbosaurus.
It is a close relative of Tyrannosaurus rex and Tarbosaurus, distinguished by its long, low skull and distinctive nasal crests.

name: Allosaurus
hints: Fossils are primarily found in the Morrison Formation, dating to the Late Jurassic period approximately 155 to 145 million years ago.
It possessed a large, lightweight skull equipped with dozens of sharp, serrated teeth designed for slicing flesh.
This large bipedal predator averaged about 8.5 meters (28 feet) in length and weighed several tons, hunting large herbivores.
It roamed what is now North America during the Late Jurassic era.
It is famously depicted in museums and media as a large, grey theropod often shown in a dynamic pose with its mouth open, sometimes appearing to attack a sauropod.

name: Alnashetri
hints: Fossils were found in the Candeleros Formation of Argentina, dating to approximately 97 million years ago during the Cenomanian stage of the Late Cretaceous.
It possessed a distinctive low ridge on the distal end of the tibia that separated the articulation surface for the astragalus from the outer face of the lateral malleolus.
This was a small coelurosaurian theropod, likely measuring around one meter in length, with notably long and slender hind limbs.
It lived in what is now South America, specifically in the region of Río Negro Province, Argentina.
Its name translates to 'slender thighs' in the Tehuelche language, referring to the elongated structure of its leg bones.

name: Alpkarakush
hints: Fossils were unearthed in the Balabansai Formation of Kyrgyzstan, dating back to the Late Jurassic period.
It possessed a distinctive, highly rugose (rough-textured) and pronounced orbital brow ridge above the eye.
This predator measured approximately 7 to 8 meters in length and was a carnivore.
It lived on the continent of Asia during the Jurassic era.
Its name is derived from a mythical powerful bird from the Epic of Manas.

name: Altirhinus
hints: Fossils were recovered from the Khuren Dukh Formation in Mongolia, dating to the Aptian to Albian stages of the Early Cretaceous, approximately 125 to 100.5 million years ago.
It possesses a distinctive tall arch on top of its snout formed by the nasal bones, which gives the genus its name meaning 'high nose'.
This herbivorous ornithopod reached an estimated length of 6.5 to 8 meters and weighed around 1.1 tonnes, using a keratinous beak and cheek teeth to process plant matter.
It lived in Asia during the Early Cretaceous period, sharing its environment with dinosaurs like Psittacosaurus and Shamosaurus.
It is an early relative of the famous duck-billed dinosaurs, sharing features like a wide muzzle and thumb spikes, but is distinguished by its unique high-arched nose.

name: Altispinax
hints: Wadhurst Clay Formation of East Sussex, England, dating to the Valanginian stage approximately 140 to 133 million years ago
Three posterior dorsal vertebrae featuring exceptionally tall neural spines that were transversely expanded and reached about thirty-five centimeters in height
A large predatory theropod estimated to weigh around one tonne and measure roughly eight meters in length
A carnivorous dinosaur that lived during the Early Cretaceous period in what is now Europe
Its name literally translates to 'with high spines,' referring to the distinctive tall, hump-like neural spines on its back vertebrae

name: Alvarezsaurus
hints: Fossils were discovered in the Bajo de la Carpa Formation in Argentina, dating to approximately 86 to 83 million years ago during the Late Cretaceous.
It possessed a unique foot structure where the third metatarsal was the longest bone, followed by an unusually long fourth metatarsal, and it lacked a fused ankle bone.
This small dinosaur measured between 1 and 1.4 meters in length, weighed roughly 3 kilograms, and was likely an insectivore.
It lived in South America during the Late Cretaceous period, making it a contemporary of other large theropods in Patagonia.
It is recognized as one of the earliest and most basal members of the alvarezsaurid family, a group of small, bird-like dinosaurs with specialized forelimbs.

name: Alwalkeria
hints: Fossils were recovered from the Lower Maleri Formation in the Godavari Valley of Andhra Pradesh, India, dating to the Carnian stage of the Late Triassic, approximately 235 to 228 million years ago.

The holotype specimen is historically problematic and considered a chimera, containing a skull likely belonging to a pseudosuchian and limb bones that have been variously interpreted as dinosaurian or lagerpetid.
It is known from fragmentary remains including a partial skull, around 28 vertebrae, and femur fragments, with no complete skeleton ever discovered.
It lived in what is now India during the Late Triassic period, an era when early archosaurs and dinosaurs were beginning to diversify.
It was originally named Walkeria in honor of paleontologist Alick Walker before being renamed Alwalkeria because the original name was already taken by a different organism.

name: Alxasaurus
hints: Fossils were recovered from the Early Cretaceous (Albian age) Bayin-Gobi Formation in Inner Mongolia, China.
It possessed long, recurved hand claws and a short tail, traits characteristic of therizinosaurs despite being an early member of the group.
This bipedal herbivore measured approximately 3.8 to 4 meters in length and weighed around 380 to 400 kilograms.
It lived in Asia during the Early Cretaceous period.
It is one of the earliest known dinosaurs with the distinctive body shape of therizinosaurs, featuring a long neck and large hand claws.

name: Amanzia
hints: Fossils were found in the Reuchenette Formation in Moutier, Switzerland, dating to the early Kimmeridgian stage, approximately 157 million years ago.
The remains consist of forty-nine bones including neck and tail vertebrae, shoulder girdle, pelvis, and limbs, with rare fossilized cartilage found in a limb joint.
It was a large, long-necked herbivorous sauropod dinosaur, with individuals varying in size by up to 20%.
It lived in Europe during the Late Jurassic period.
It is the first sauropod dinosaur to be named from fossil remains discovered in Switzerland.

name: Amargasaurus
hints: Fossils were found in the La Amarga Formation of Neuquén Province, Argentina, dating to the Barremian-Aptian stages of the Early Cretaceous (approximately 129–122 million years ago).
It possessed unique bifurcated neural spines on its neck and back vertebrae that formed two parallel rows, which were taller than those of any other known sauropod.
This sauropod was relatively small for its group, measuring 9 to 13 meters long and weighing around 2.6 to 4 metric tons, and it was a herbivore that likely fed at mid-height.
It lived in South America during the Early Cretaceous period.
It is instantly recognizable by the double row of extremely tall, backward-bowing spines running along its neck and back.

name: Amargatitanis
hints: Fossils were discovered in the La Amarga Formation of Neuquén, Argentina, dating to the Barremian stage of the Early Cretaceous, approximately 129 to 123 million years ago.
It possessed tall neural spines extending from its vertebrae and a femur that is significantly wider at the upper end than the lower end, a trait distinguishing it from many other sauropods.
This dinosaur was a small sauropod, reaching approximately 12 meters in length and weighing around 3.5 metric tons, and it was a herbivore.
It lived in what is now South America during the Early Cretaceous period.
It is a member of the Dicraeosauridae family, a group of sauropods known for having shorter necks compared to other long-necked dinosaurs.

name: Amazonsaurus
hints: Fossils were discovered in the Itapecuru Formation of Maranhão, Brazil, dating to the Aptian through Albian epochs of the Early Cretaceous, approximately 125 to 100 million years ago.
It possessed tall neural spines on its tail vertebrae, a distinctive anatomical feature that identifies it as a diplodocoid sauropod.
This large-bodied quadrupedal herbivore was estimated to be about 12 meters (40 feet) long and weighed around 5,000 kilograms.
It lived in South America during the Early Cretaceous period, specifically in what is now the Amazon Basin region.
It is recognized as the first named dinosaur genus from the territory of the Amazon Basin.

name: Ambopteryx
hints: Fossils were recovered from the stratigraphic equivalent of the Haifanggou Formation in Liaoning Province, China, dating to the Oxfordian stage of the Late Jurassic.
It possessed a unique 'styliform element,' an elongated bone extending from the wrist, which supported a bat-like wing membrane stretching from the third finger to the abdomen.
This small dinosaur measured approximately 32 centimeters in length and weighed around 306 grams, and evidence of gastroliths and bone fragments suggests it was omnivorous.
It lived in what is now China during the Late Jurassic period, part of the Yanliao Biota ecosystem.
It is one of only two known dinosaurs to have possessed both traditional feathers and membranous, bat-like wings.

name: Ampelognathus
hints: The fossil was discovered in the Lewisville Formation of Texas, dating back to the Cenomanian stage of the Late Cretaceous, approximately 99 to 94 million years ago.
The only known specimen is a single, nearly-complete left dentary (jawbone), which is the sole basis for identifying this genus.
It was a small, herbivorous ornithopod dinosaur, likely related to iguanodontians, though its exact size remains estimated due to the limited fossil material.
This dinosaur lived in what is now North America during the Late Cretaceous period.
Its name translates to 'grapevine jaw,' a reference to the fact that the fossil was found near Grapevine Lake in Texas.

name: Ampelosaurus
hints: Fossils were recovered from the Marnes Rouges Inférieures Formation, dating to approximately 71.5 million years ago in the early Maastrichtian stage.
This sauropod is distinct among its kind for possessing armor in the form of osteoderms embedded in its skin.
It was a large herbivore, with the biggest known individuals reaching about 14 meters in length.
It lived during the Late Cretaceous period on the Ibero-Armorican Island, which corresponds to modern-day France and the Iberian Peninsula.
Its name translates to 'vine lizard,' referencing the vineyards of the region where its bones were first discovered.

name: Amphicoelias
hints: Fossils were discovered in the Late Jurassic Morrison Formation of Colorado, specifically near Cañon City.
It is a diplodocid sauropod characterized by amphicoelous vertebrae, meaning both ends of the backbone bones are concave.
It was a massive herbivore, estimated to reach lengths of around 18 meters and weigh approximately 15 metric tons.
It lived in what is now North America during the Late Jurassic period, roughly 150 million years ago.
It is a long-necked, four-legged plant-eater with a whip-like tail, belonging to the same family as Diplodocus.

name: Amtocephale
hints: Its fossils were found in the Baynshire Formation at the Amtgai locality in the Gobi Desert, dating to approximately 95.9 to 89.6 million years ago.
It is known primarily from a nearly complete frontoparietal dome where the parietal bone contributes an exceptionally large 41% of the total length.
As a pachycephalosaurid, it was a small, herbivorous dinosaur with a thick, bony skull roof.
It lived during the Late Cretaceous period in what is now Mongolia, Asia.
It is best recognized for its distinctive, thick, bony dome on top of its head.

name: Amurosaurus
hints: Fossils were recovered from the Udurchukan Formation in the Amur Oblast of far eastern Russia, dating to approximately 68 million years ago.
It possessed a hollow crest on its skull, a characteristic feature of lambeosaurine dinosaurs, and had a distinctive sigmoidal (S-shaped) ulna bone.
This herbivorous dinosaur grew to an estimated length of 8 meters and weighed around 3 metric tons.
It lived during the Late Cretaceous period in what is now eastern Asia, specifically near the border of modern-day Russia and China.
It is a type of duck-billed dinosaur known for the elaborate hollow crest on its head, similar to a Parasaurolophus or Corythosaurus.

name: Amygdalodon
hints: Fossils were discovered in the Toarcian-aged Cerro Carnerero Formation in Argentina, dating back approximately 180 to 172 million years.
It is a basal sauropod distinguished by its unique almond-shaped teeth, which gave the genus its name.
This quadrupedal herbivore reached an estimated length of 12 meters and weighed over 5 tonnes.
It lived during the Early Jurassic period on the continent of South America.
It is recognized as one of the few known Jurassic dinosaurs found in South America.

name: Anabisetia
hints: Fossils were found in the Cerro Lisandro Formation of Argentina, dating to approximately 95 to 92 million years ago.
It possessed the largest acromial process on the shoulder blade ever recorded among Euornithopods.
It was a small, bipedal herbivore measuring about 2 meters in length and weighing roughly 20 kilograms.
It lived in South America during the Late Cretaceous period.
It is a small, bird-hipped plant-eater from Patagonia.

name: Analong
hints: Fossils were discovered in the Chuanjie Formation in Yunnan, China, dating to the Middle Jurassic period approximately 174 million years ago.

It is a mamenchisaurid sauropod distinguished by unique skeletal features, including transverse processes on its first ten tail vertebrae and a specific 45-degree angle between processes on its ulna bone.
This was a giant herbivorous dinosaur that possessed an extremely long neck and a massive body, typical of its family of long-necked plant-eaters.
It lived in what is now China during the Middle Jurassic epoch, sharing its environment with other large dinosaurs like Shidaisaurus and Chuanjiesaurus.
It is a massive, long-necked dinosaur from China, often recognized by its immense size and the distinctive long tail characteristic of mamenchisaurids.

name: Anasazisaurus
hints: Fossils were discovered in the Farmington Member of the Kirtland Formation in the San Juan Basin of New Mexico, dating to approximately 74 million years ago.
It possessed a distinctive short, tab-like nasal crest that rose between and above the eyes and folded back under itself, distinguishing it from other duck-billed dinosaurs.
This herbivorous ornithopod was approximately 7.5 meters long and weighed around 2,500 kilograms, using dental batteries to grind plant material.
It lived in North America during the Late Cretaceous period, sharing its environment with other dinosaurs like Triceratops and Tyrannosaurus.
It is a 'duck-billed' dinosaur known for having a low, solid crest on its head rather than a long, hollow tube.

name: Anchiceratops
hints: Fossils are primarily found in the Horseshoe Canyon Formation of Alberta, dating to approximately 72.5-71 million years ago.
It possessed a distinctive rectangular skull frill edged with exceptionally wide, coarse, triangular bony projections called epoccipitals.
This medium-sized quadrupedal herbivore reached an estimated length of 4.3 meters and weighed around 1.2 tonnes, feeding on plants.
It lived in what is now North America during the Late Cretaceous Period.
It is a horned dinosaur with two long brow horns and a shorter nose horn, resembling a close relative of Triceratops.

name: Anchiornis
hints: Found in the Tiaojishan Formation of Liaoning, China, dating to the Late Jurassic approximately 160 million years ago
Possessed long feathers on its arms, legs, and tail, giving it a distinctive four-winged appearance
A small, crow-sized bipedal dinosaur that weighed roughly 110 grams and likely ate insects or small prey
Lived in what is now China during the Late Jurassic period
Famous for being one of the first dinosaurs with its original colors and shaggy feather patterns scientifically reconstructed

name: Anchisaurus
hints: Fossils are found in the Upper Portland Formation of the northeastern United States, dating to the Hettangian to Sinemurian stages of the Early Jurassic, approximately 200 to 192 million years ago.
It is a basal sauropodomorph with a distinctive large thumb, which originally led to its provisional naming as Megadactylus.
This dinosaur was quite small for a dinosaur, measuring just over 2 meters in length and weighing around 27 kilograms, and likely had an omnivorous diet including gastroliths.
It lived in what is now North America during the Early Jurassic period.
It was one of the first dinosaurs discovered in North America, and its initial remains were so small that they were mistakenly thought to be human bones.

name: Andesaurus
hints: Fossils were discovered in the Candeleros Formation of Neuquén Province, Argentina, dating to the Cenomanian stage of the Late Cretaceous, approximately 99.6 to 97 million years ago.

It is a basal titanosaurian sauropod characterized by tall neural spines on its back vertebrae and amphiplatyan (flat-ended) tail vertebrae, distinguishing it from more derived titanosaurs with ball-and-socket tail joints.
This medium-sized sauropod measured between 15 and 18 meters in length and weighed around 7 metric tons, feeding on vegetation as a herbivore.
It lived in South America during the middle of the Cretaceous Period.
It is known as the 'Andes lizard' and is recognized as one of the most basal members of the Titanosauria clade.

name: Angolatitan
hints: Fossils were found in the Tadi Beds of the Itombe Formation, dating to the Coniacian stage of the Late Cretaceous, approximately 89 to 86 million years ago.
It is a basal titanosauriform with a less robust forelimb than derived titanosaurs, featuring slender, equal-length metacarpals and an absent olecranon.
As a sauropod, it was a massive, long-necked herbivore, though only partial forelimb bones have been discovered.
It lived in what is now Angola, Africa, in a desert-like ecosystem during the Late Cretaceous period.
It was the first non-avian dinosaur ever discovered in Angola.

name: Angulomastacator
hints: Fossils were found in the upper shale member of the Aguja Formation in Big Bend National Park, Texas, dating to approximately 76.6 million years ago.
It possessed a unique partial upper jaw (maxilla) that curves downward at a sharp angle of about 45 degrees at the front, unlike any other known duck-billed dinosaur.
This herbivorous dinosaur was a lambeosaurine hadrosaur, meaning it had a hollow crest, and it likely walked on both two and four legs.
It lived during the Late Cretaceous period on the continent of North America.
It is the only known dinosaur with a distinctly bent or downturned snout, earning it the nickname 'bend chewer'.

name: Anhuilong
hints: Fossils were discovered in the Hongqin Formation of Anhui province, China, dating to the Late Jurassic period approximately 150 million years ago.
The known remains consist of a forelimb including a humerus, ulna, and radius, with the ulna exhibiting a distinctive elliptical cross-section that is unique among its close relatives.
This dinosaur was a massive long-necked herbivore, likely reaching lengths of around 15 meters and weighing several tons as it browsed on high vegetation.
It lived in East Asia during the Late Jurassic epoch, a time when giant sauropods were thriving across the supercontinent of Laurasia.
It is a type of mamenchisaurid, a group of sauropods famous for possessing some of the longest necks relative to body size of any known land animal.

name: Aniksosaurus
hints: Fossils were recovered from the Bajo Barreal Formation in Chubut Province, Argentina, dating to the Cenomanian-Turonian stages of the Late Cretaceous, approximately 96 to 91 million years ago.
It possessed a uniquely extremely expanded brevis shelf on its ilium, which served as a large attachment site for tail muscles.
This small theropod measured between 2 and 3 meters in length and weighed roughly 35 to 45 kilograms, indicating it was a lightweight, agile predator.
It lived in Patagonia, South America, during the Late Cretaceous period.
Its name translates to 'spring lizard,' referencing the season when the first fossils were discovered in 1995.

name: Animantarx
hints: Fossils were discovered in the Mussentuchit Member of the Cedar Mountain Formation in Utah, dating to approximately 106 to 97 million years ago, and were initially located by a radiological survey due to slightly radioactive remains.
This dinosaur is a nodosaurid ankylosaur characterized by a highly domed skull, small horns on the postorbital and quadratojugal bones, and a mandible that is armored on only half of its length.
It was a small, slow-moving quadrupedal herbivore, measuring no more than 3 meters (10 feet) in length, and unlike some relatives, it lacked a tail club.
It lived during the Late Cretaceous period on the continent of North America.
It is known as the 'living citadel' due to its heavy armor scutes that made it look like an unassailable fortress.

name: Ankylosaurus
hints: Fossils have been found in the Hell Creek, Lance, Scollard, Frenchman, and Ferris formations, dating to approximately 68–66 million years ago.
It possessed a large, bony club at the end of its tail and was covered in armor plates called osteoderms.
It was a quadrupedal herbivore estimated to be between 6 and 8 meters long and weighing up to 8 tons.
It lived in western North America during the very end of the Cretaceous Period.
It is famous for its heavy armor and the distinctive bone club on its tail, often depicted as a tank-like dinosaur.

name: Anodontosaurus
hints: Fossils are found in the Horseshoe Canyon Formation of southern Alberta, Canada, dating to approximately 72.8-67 million years ago.
It possesses a wide, pointed tail club and distinctive pointed, triangular knob osteoderms on its tail.
This medium-sized quadrupedal herbivore measured up to 5 meters in length and weighed around 2 metric tons.
It lived during the Late Cretaceous period on the continent of North America.
It is a heavily armored dinosaur with a bony tail club, resembling a living tank.

name: Anomalipes
hints: Fossils were found in the Wangshi Group of China, dating to the Campanian stage of the Late Cretaceous, specifically at the Kugou locality.
It possessed a distinctive hindlimb with an unusually shaped foot, which is the feature that inspired its name meaning 'unusual foot'.
This was a small, bipedal dinosaur that likely fed on plants or small animals, known only from partial leg bones.
It lived in what is now Asia during the Late Cretaceous period.
It is an oviraptorosaur, a group of beaked, feathered dinosaurs often mistaken for bird-like theropods.

name: Anoplosaurus
hints: Fossils were discovered in the Cambridge Greensand formation of Cambridgeshire, England, dating to the late Albian age of the Early Cretaceous, approximately 100 million years ago.
It is a nodosaurid ankylosaur characterized by a lack of external armor plates, a feature that may be due to the specimen being a juvenile or representing a primitive basal form.
This quadrupedal herbivore was relatively small for its group, with the original type specimen estimated to be only about five feet in length.
It lived in what is now England during the Early Cretaceous period, a time when the region was part of a warm, shallow sea environment.
Its name literally translates to 'unarmored lizard,' reflecting the surprising absence of the bony armor typically associated with its ankylosaur relatives.

name: Anserimimus
hints: Fossils were discovered in the Nemegt Formation of Mongolia, dating to the early Maastrichtian stage of the Late Cretaceous, approximately 70 million years ago.
It possessed unusually powerful forelimbs with large muscle attachment crests on the shoulder and upper arm, along with distinctive flattened, straight claws on its hands.
This medium-sized dinosaur measured about 3 meters (9.8 feet) in length and weighed between 50 and 170 kilograms, likely feeding on an omnivorous diet.
It lived in what is now Mongolia during the Late Cretaceous period, sharing its habitat with giants like Tarbosaurus and Deinocheirus.
Its name means 'goose mimic,' reflecting its classification as an ornithomimid, a group of bird-like dinosaurs known for their long legs and speed.

name: Antarctopelta
hints: Fossils were discovered in the Gamma Member of the Snow Hill Island Formation, dating to the Maastrichtian stage of the Late Cretaceous period.
It possessed a unique tail weapon called a macuahuitl, consisting of a row of spiked osteoderms resembling an Aztec club, along with spikes above its eyes.
This medium-sized herbivore reached approximately 4 to 6 meters in length and weighed around 350 kilograms.
It lived on the continent of Antarctica during the Late Cretaceous period.
It is the only known armored dinosaur (ankylosaur) ever discovered on the continent of Antarctica.

name: Antarctosaurus
hints: Fossils attributed to this genus have been found in the Anacleto Formation of Argentina and the Plottier Formation, dating to the Late Cretaceous period.
It is a titanosaurian sauropod characterized by a squared-off lower jaw with L-shaped dentary bones and small, slender teeth restricted to the front of the jaw.
This massive quadrupedal herbivore is estimated to have reached lengths of around 17 meters and weighed approximately 34 metric tons.
It lived during the Late Cretaceous period on the continent of South America, specifically in what is now Argentina.
It is one of the largest dinosaurs known, with the name translating to 'southern lizard' despite not being found on the continent of Antarctica.

name: Anteavis
hints: Fossils were found in the Cancha de Bochas Member of the Ischigualasto Formation in northwestern Argentina, dating to the Carnian stage of the Late Triassic, approximately 231 to 228 million years ago.
The specific name refers to its proportionally elongate lower hindlimb bones, indicating a build adapted for speed or agility.
This was a small, bipedal carnivore that likely measured around one meter in length and weighed only a few kilograms.
It lived during the Late Triassic period on the supercontinent of Gondwana, specifically in what is now South America.
It is known as one of the earliest known members of the theropod dinosaur lineage, representing a primitive ancestor to the group that includes birds.

name: Antetonitrus
hints: Fossils were recovered from the Early Jurassic Upper Elliot Formation, dating to approximately 201 to 196 million years ago.
It possessed a flexible, grasping thumb on its forelimb, a primitive trait lost in later, fully quadrupedal sauropods.
It was a quadrupedal herbivore measuring approximately 8 to 10 meters in length and weighing around 5.6 metric tons.
It lived during the Early Jurassic period on the supercontinent of Pangaea, in what is now South Africa.
Its name translates to 'before thunder,' signifying its status as one of the earliest known sauropods.

name: Aoniraptor
hints: Found in the Huincul Formation of Argentina, dating to the Late Cretaceous period approximately 90 million years ago
A large theropod with a distinctive pneumatic structure in its tail vertebrae, distinguishing it from similar megaraptorans like Gualicho
Measuring up to 6 meters (20 feet) in length, this carnivorous dinosaur was a medium-sized predator
Lived in what is now South America during the final era of the dinosaurs
Its name translates to 'Southern Thief', reflecting its classification as a raptor-like meat-eater from the southern hemisphere

name: Aorun
hints: Fossils were recovered from the Shishugou Formation in the Junggar Basin of Xinjiang, China, dating to approximately 161.6 million years ago.
It possesses a distinctive skull opening called the fenestra maxillaris located in front of the fenestra antorbitalis, and its hand claws vary significantly in size and shape.
This was a small, bipedal carnivore; the known specimen is a juvenile measuring about 1 meter in length and weighing roughly 1.5 kilograms.
It lived in Asia during the Late Jurassic period, a time when fossils of its specific group of theropods are exceptionally rare.
It is recognized as one of the oldest known members of the coelurosaur lineage, representing an early stage in the evolution of the group that would eventually include birds.

name: Apatoraptor
hints: Found in the Campanian-aged Horseshoe Canyon Formation of Alberta, specifically the Horsethief Member
Possesses quill knobs on the ulna, indicating it had wings and was covered in feathers
A small theropod dinosaur that likely weighed only a few kilograms and ate a varied diet
Lived in North America during the Late Cretaceous period
Known as the 'deceptive thief' because its fossil was initially misidentified as an ornithomimid for over a decade

name: Apatosaurus
hints: Fossils are found in the Morrison Formation, dating to approximately 152–151 million years ago in the Late Jurassic.
It possessed a robust, deep neck with stout cervical vertebrae and a skull that was once mistakenly mounted on its body as a cast of a different dinosaur.
This massive herbivore reached lengths of up to 30 meters and weighed around 33 tons, using its long neck to browse on high vegetation.
It roamed across what is now North America during the Late Jurassic period.
It is one of the most recognizable long-necked dinosaurs, often confused with or compared to Brontosaurus due to its similar massive build and whip-like tail.

name: Appalachiosaurus
hints: Fossils were discovered in the Demopolis Chalk Formation of Alabama, dating to approximately 77 million years ago during the Campanian stage of the Late Cretaceous.
It possessed a distinctive row of six low crests along the top of its snout and exhibited a bone-crushing bite force of around 32,500 newtons.
This bipedal predator was a basal member of the tyrannosauroid lineage, with the known juvenile specimen measuring about 6.5 meters long and weighing roughly 623 kilograms.
It lived on the ancient island continent of Appalachia, which corresponds to the eastern region of modern-day North America.
It is an early relative of the famous Tyrannosaurus rex, representing a 'primitive' or basal stage in the evolution of the giant apex predators.

name: Aquilarhinus
hints: Lower Shale of the Aguja Formation in Big Bend National Park, Texas, dating to the Late Cretaceous period approximately 70-73 million years ago
Possessed a unique, shovel-like beak morphology on its lower jaw, distinct from the typical beaks of other duck-billed dinosaurs
A medium-sized herbivorous ornithopod that likely fed on low-lying vegetation using its specialized scooping beak
Lived in what is now North America during the Late Cretaceous period
Known for its unusual, spade-shaped lower beak that looks like a shovel

name: Aquilops
hints: Fossils were discovered in the Cloverly Formation of Montana, dating to the Albian stage of the Early Cretaceous, approximately 109 to 104 million years ago.
It possesses a distinctive downward-curving rostral bone that forms the core of its beaked snout, featuring an arched keel on top.
This small dinosaur measured about 60 centimeters in length and weighed roughly 1.5 kilograms, and it was a herbivore.
It lived in North America during the Early Cretaceous period.
It is recognized as one of the earliest and most primitive horned dinosaurs (ceratopsians) found in North America.

name: Arackar
hints: Fossils were discovered in the Hornitos Formation of Chile, dating to the Late Cretaceous period (Campanian-Maastrichtian stages).
It is a lithostrotian sauropod with distinctive vertebral features, including high and wide hollowing between the front joint process and the vertebral body.
The known juvenile specimen was approximately 6.3 meters long, and it was a herbivore.
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Late Cretaceous period.
It is a type of armored or nodosaur-like sauropod from Chile, often noted for being one of the most complete sauropod specimens found in that country.

name: Aragosaurus
hints: Fossils were discovered in the Villar del Arzobispo Formation, dating to the Early Cretaceous period approximately 145 to 140 million years ago.
It possessed a short, compact skull with large, wide teeth adapted for slicing vegetation, and forelimbs that were only slightly shorter than its hind limbs.
This massive herbivore reached an estimated length of 18 meters and weighed around 25 tonnes.
It lived on the continent of Europe during the Early Cretaceous period.
It was a large, four-legged plant-eater with a long neck and a bulky body, closely resembling the American dinosaur Camarasaurus.

name: Aralosaurus
hints: Fossils were recovered from the Bostobe Formation in Kazakhstan, dating to approximately 83.6 million years ago during the Upper Santonian to lower Campanian stages.
It possesses a hollow bony structure in the nasal bone located just in front of the eye sockets, which is a primitive characteristic of the subfamily Lambeosaurinae.
This herbivore was a relatively small hadrosaur, estimated to be around 6.5 meters in length, with the known specimen likely being an immature individual.
It lived in Central Asia during the Late Cretaceous period, making it one of the few known lambeosaurines from that specific region and time.
It is a crested duck-billed dinosaur, distinguished by its hollow nasal crest that surrounded part of its respiratory system.

name: Aratasaurus
hints: Fossils were found in the lower levels of the Romualdo Formation in Ceará, Brazil, dating to approximately 115 million years ago during the Aptian stage.
The only known specimen is a partial right leg, including parts of the femur, tibia, and foot bones, suggesting it was a juvenile or young adult.
It was a small, bipedal carnivore with an estimated length of about 3.12 meters and a weight of roughly 34 kilograms.
This dinosaur lived in what is now northeastern Brazil during the Early Cretaceous period.
Its scientific name, Aratasaurus museunacionali, translates to 'the dinosaur born from the National Museum fire,' honoring the specimen's survival of the 2018 fire at the National Museum of Brazil.

name: Archaeoceratops
hints: Fossils were discovered in the Middle Gray Unit of the Xinminbao Group within the Gongpoquan Basin of Gansu Province, dating to the Aptian stage of the Early Cretaceous approximately 125 million years ago.
It possessed a small bony frill at the back of its skull but completely lacked the horns characteristic of later relatives in its group.
This dinosaur was remarkably small for a horned dinosaur, measuring only about 0.9 meters (3 feet) in length and weighing around 10 kilograms (22 pounds).
It lived in what is now north central China during the Early Cretaceous period.
It is recognized as one of the smallest known ceratopsians, often described as a tiny, bipedal, hornless ancestor of the famous Triceratops.

name: Archaeocursor
hints: Fossils were found in the Ziliujing Formation in China, dating to the Early Jurassic period approximately 200 million years ago.
The only known specimen is a single, nearly complete left femur measuring 93 millimeters in length.
It was a small dinosaur, estimated to be about 1 meter (3.3 feet) long, and as an ornithischian, it likely ate plants.
It lived in Asia during the Early Jurassic, making it the oldest and most basal ornithischian described from that continent.
Its name translates to 'old runner,' reflecting its status as an ancient, early-diverging member of the bird-hipped dinosaur group.

name: Archaeodontosaurus
hints: Fossils were recovered from the Isalo III Formation in Madagascar, dating to the Middle Jurassic period approximately 174 to 163 million years ago.
This dinosaur possessed distinctive, chisel-shaped teeth that resemble those of prosauropods, which is unusual for a typical sauropod.
It was a large, long-necked herbivorous quadruped, likely reaching lengths of around 10 to 12 meters.
It lived in the ancient supercontinent of Gondwana, specifically on the island that is now Madagascar, during the Jurassic era.
It is known as an 'ancient-toothed lizard' due to its unique dental structure that looks like it belongs to an earlier group of dinosaurs.

name: Archaeornithoides
hints: Found in the Late Cretaceous Djadokhta Formation beds of Mongolia, dating to the late Campanian stage approximately 72 to 74 million years ago.
Possessed small, conical, and smooth teeth that completely lacked serrations or carinae, along with a long antorbital fenestra stretching over three quarters of the maxilla.
Was a very small theropod dinosaur with an estimated body length of only fifty to sixty centimeters, making it one of the smallest known non-avian dinosaurs.
Lived during the Late Cretaceous period in the continent of Asia, specifically within the fossil-rich regions of modern-day Mongolia.
Often considered by early researchers to be a close relative of birds or an ancient bird-like dinosaur due to its unique skeletal features.

name: Archaeornithomimus
hints: Fossils were discovered in the Iren Dabasu Formation of Inner Mongolia, China, dating to approximately 96 million years ago during the Cenomanian stage.
It possessed a keratinous beak and highly pneumatized cervical vertebrae with complex internal chambers, but its foot was not arctometatarsalian.
This medium-sized theropod reached about 3.4 meters (11 feet) in length and weighed over 71.5 kilograms (158 pounds), likely acting as an omnivore.
It lived in Asia during the Late Cretaceous period, sharing its floodplain habitat with dinosaurs like Bactrosaurus and Alectrosaurus.
It is known as an 'ancient bird mimic' due to its long legs and bird-like appearance, resembling a giant ostrich.

name: Arcovenator
hints: Fossils were discovered in the Lower Argiles Rutilantes Formation in southeastern France, dating to the Late Campanian stage, approximately 72 to 76 million years ago.
It possessed a distinctive midline foramen on the skull roof, likely for the pineal gland, and a thick, rough-surfaced bony brow ridge extending above the eye socket.
This abelisaurid theropod was a medium-sized carnivore, estimated to reach lengths of about 4.8 to 6 meters.
It lived during the Late Cretaceous period on the landmasses that are now part of Europe, specifically in what is now France.
It is a close relative of Majungasaurus and Carnotaurus, belonging to a group of theropods known for having tiny, vestigial forelimbs and short, tall skulls.

name: Arcusaurus
hints: Fossils were discovered in the upper Elliot Formation in Senekal, Free State, South Africa, dating to the Pliensbachian stage of the Early Jurassic.
It possesses unique bony shelves on the inner side of the premaxilla that form the front part of the palate, along with a large elliptical opening penetrating this palate.
This basal sauropodomorph was relatively small, with the known specimens representing juveniles, and it was a herbivore.
It lived in what is now South Africa during the Early Jurassic period.
It is a primitive, long-necked dinosaur relative of the giant sauropods, often recognized by its early evolutionary position among plant-eating dinosaurs.

name: Ardetosaurus
hints: Fossils were recovered from the Howe Quarry in the Morrison Formation of northern Wyoming, dating to the Kimmeridgian stage approximately 152 million years ago.
It is a diplodocid sauropod characterized by an extremely elongated neck, a whip-like tail, and thin, pencil-shaped teeth confined to the front of the jaws.
This large, quadrupedal herbivore is estimated to have reached lengths of around 18 meters, with a femur measuring approximately 130 centimeters.
It lived during the Late Jurassic period on the continent of North America.
It possesses a long neck and a very long, thin tail that resembles a whip.

name: Arenysaurus
hints: Found in the late Maastrichtian-age Tremp Formation of the Pyrenees Mountains in Spain, dating to approximately 66 million years ago
Possessed a hollow and decorative cranial crest, a distinctive feature of the lambeosaurine subfamily
A medium-sized herbivore measuring 5–6 metres (16–20 ft) long and weighing around 1 metric ton
Lived in Europe during the Late Cretaceous period, making it one of the last non-avian dinosaurs before the extinction event
Recognized as one of the most complete and best-dated hadrosaurid skeletons ever discovered from the very end of the dinosaur era

name: Argentinosaurus
hints: Fossils were recovered from the Huincul Formation in Argentina, dating to approximately 97 to 93.5 million years ago.
It possessed massive dorsal vertebrae and a sacrum with fused vertebrae that were encased in extremely hard rock, requiring pneumatic hammers for excavation.
This giant sauropod measured between 30 and 35 meters long and weighed between 65 and 100 tonnes, making it one of the largest land animals ever.
It lived during the Late Cretaceous period on the continent of South America.
It is widely recognized as one of the biggest dinosaurs ever discovered, often cited as the largest known land animal.

name: Argyrosaurus
hints: Fossils were found in the Lago Colhué Huapi Formation, dating to the early late Maastrichtian stage of the Late Cretaceous, approximately 70 million years ago.
It is distinguished by a unique humerus featuring a wide ridge on the medial side that projects forward and an extremely compressed midshaft.
This titanosaurian sauropod was a massive herbivore, with length estimates ranging from 17 to 28 meters and weights up to 50 tonnes.
It lived in what is now South America, specifically in the region of modern-day Argentina.
Its name translates to 'silver lizard,' a reference to the meaning of Argentina, which is 'land of silver.'

name: Aristosuchus
hints: Fossils were discovered in the Wessex Formation on the Isle of Wight, dating to the Barremian stage of the Early Cretaceous, approximately 130 to 123 million years ago.
It possessed a distinctive femur featuring a wing-like anterior trochanter and a markedly reduced fourth trochanter.
This was a small, bipedal carnivorous theropod, estimated to be about 2 meters long and weighing between 7 and 30 kilograms.
It lived during the Early Cretaceous period on what is now the island of Wight in England, UK.
It is a very small, agile meat-eating dinosaur from the Early Cretaceous, originally mistaken for a crocodile relative due to its name meaning 'best crocodile'.

name: Arkansaurus
hints: Fossils were discovered in the Early Cretaceous Trinity Group at a road construction site near Lockesburg, Arkansas.
It possessed a laterally compressed third metatarsal and strong, curved foot claws, unlike the flat claws of many related species.
This bipedal dinosaur measured approximately 4.6 meters (15 feet) in length and weighed around 380 kilograms (839 pounds).
It lived during the Early Cretaceous period in what is now North America.
It is the only officially described dinosaur species found in the state of Arkansas.

name: Arrhinoceratops
hints: Fossils were discovered in the Horseshoe Canyon Formation in Alberta, Canada, dating to the latest Campanian or earliest Maastrichtian stage of the Late Cretaceous.
It possessed a broad, square neck frill with two oval-shaped openings and a nose horn that was shorter and blunter than those of its famous relative Triceratops.
This herbivore is estimated to have reached a length of approximately 4.5 to 6 meters and weighed around 1.3 tonnes, feeding on ferns, cycads, and conifers.
It lived in North America during the Late Cretaceous period, roughly 70 to 66 million years ago.
It is a horned dinosaur closely related to Triceratops and Anchiceratops, known for its distinctive short face and lack of a prominent nose horn.

name: Arrudatitan
hints: Fossils were recovered from the Adamantina Formation in Brazil, dating to the Late Cretaceous period (Campanian-Maastrichtian stages).
This sauropod possessed a relatively gracile skeletal structure compared to other titanosaurians.
It reached an estimated length of 15 meters (49 feet) and was a herbivore.
It lived in South America during the final era of the dinosaurs.
It is a massive, long-necked plant-eating dinosaur with a tail and four pillar-like legs.

name: Asfaltovenator
hints: Fossils were recovered from the Cañadón Asfalto Formation in Chubut Province, Argentina, dating to the Middle Toarcian stage approximately 178 million years ago.
The skull features a distinctive supraantorbital crest on the lacrimal bone and unique horizontal ridges on the exoccipital bone at the rear of the skull.
It was a large carnivorous theropod estimated to reach 7 to 8 meters in length, comparable in size to Allosaurus.
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Early Jurassic period.
It is a basal member of the allosauroid group, representing an early branch of the lineage that includes large predators like Allosaurus.

name: Asiaceratops
hints: Fossils of the type species were found in the Khodzhakul Formation in Uzbekistan, dating to approximately 99 million years ago.
It possessed a sharp, parrot-like beak characteristic of the Ceratopsia group, though it lacked the large frills or horns seen in later relatives.
This was a small, herbivorous dinosaur that likely fed on ferns, cycads, and conifers.
It lived in Asia during the Early to Late Cretaceous period.
It is known as one of the earliest and most primitive members of the horned dinosaur family tree.

name: Asiatyrannus
hints: Fossils were found in the Nanxiong Formation of Jiangxi Province, China, dating to the very end of the Maastrichtian age, approximately 66 million years ago.
It possessed a notably deep and robust skull snout, which distinguishes it from the long, narrow snouts of other contemporary Asian tyrannosaurs.
This was a small-to-medium sized predator, with an estimated body length of only 3.5 to 4 meters, making it significantly smaller than its famous relatives.
It lived in Asia during the Late Cretaceous period, representing the southernmost known record of a tyrannosaurid dinosaur on that continent.
It is a member of the tyrannosaur family, closely related to the 'Asian T-Rex' Tarbosaurus, and shares the characteristic short arms and massive skull of its group.

name: Astigmasaura
hints: Fossils were found in the lower Huincul Formation of Neuquén Province, Argentina, dating to the Cenomanian age approximately 99 million years ago.
