It is a rebbachisaurid sauropod, a group known for having reduced or absent teeth and often possessing osteoderms or bony armor plates.
As a large herbivorous sauropod, it would have been a long-necked plant-eater, likely reaching lengths of 10 to 15 meters.
It lived in South America during the Late Cretaceous period.
It is a long-necked, four-legged plant-eating dinosaur, similar in general shape to a Brachiosaurus but with a different neck structure and lack of teeth.

name: Astrophocaudia
hints: Fossils were discovered in the Paluxy Formation of Texas, dating to the Albian stage of the Early Cretaceous, approximately 100 to 113 million years ago.
It is distinguished by unique features in its tail vertebrae, specifically a 'non-twisting' articulation structure and a cross-section that resembles a three-pointed star.
Estimates suggest it was a large, long-necked plant-eater measuring between 11 and 18 meters in length and weighing up to 20 tons.
It lived in what is now North America during the Early Cretaceous period.
It is a massive, four-legged herbivore with a very long neck and tail, representing the classic sauropod body plan.

name: Asylosaurus
hints: Fossils were discovered in the Late Triassic Avon Fissure Fill at Durdham Down, Clifton, Bristol, dating to the Rhaetian age.
It is distinguished from contemporaneous relatives like Thecodontosaurus by the unique structure of its humerus (upper arm bone).
This basal sauropodomorph was approximately 2 meters (6.7 feet) long and weighed around 250 kilograms (550 pounds), likely having an omnivorous or herbivorous diet.
It lived in what is now England during the Late Triassic period.
It is a small, early dinosaur from the Triassic period, often confused with or compared to Thecodontosaurus due to their similar builds and close relationship.

name: Atacamatitan
hints: Fossils were discovered in the Tolar Formation, located in the Atacama Desert of Chile, with a geological age ranging from the Late Cretaceous to the Paleocene.

It is a titanosaurian sauropod characterized by dorsal vertebrae with rounded pleurocoels that do not point posteriorly and posterior caudal vertebrae with laterally compressed, blade-like neural spines.
This massive herbivore is estimated to have weighed between 8 and 16 metric tons, with a femur measuring approximately 1.10 meters long.
It lived in South America during the Late Cretaceous period.
It is a giant, long-necked, four-legged plant-eating dinosaur known as a titanosaur.

name: Athenar
hints: Fossils were recovered from the Carnegie Quarry at Dinosaur National Monument in Utah, specifically within the Morrison Formation dating to approximately 156.3 to 146.8 million years ago.
It possesses distinctive skull features including frontoparietal and postparietal fenestrae (holes) and a unique prong on the squamosal bone.
As a dicraeosaurid sauropod, it was characterized by a relatively smaller size and a shorter neck compared to its long-necked relatives like Diplodocus.
It lived in North America during the Late Jurassic period, sharing its ecosystem with famous dinosaurs such as Allosaurus, Stegosaurus, and Apatosaurus.
It is a long-necked, four-legged plant-eating dinosaur that was originally misidentified as a species of Diplodocus before being reclassified in 2025.

name: Atlasaurus
hints: Fossils were found in the Guettioua Formation of Morocco, dating to the Middle Jurassic period (Bathonian to Callovian stages).
It possesses a proportionately larger skull and a shorter neck compared to its relative Brachiosaurus, along with spoon-shaped teeth featuring denticles.
This dinosaur reached an estimated length of 15 meters (49 feet) and weighed approximately 22.5 tonnes, feeding on vegetation.
It lived in North Africa during the Middle Jurassic era.
Its name means 'Atlas lizard,' referencing the Atlas Mountains of Morocco where it was discovered and its giant size.

name: Atlascopcosaurus
hints: Found in the Early Cretaceous (Aptian-Albian) layers of the Eumeralla Formation at Dinosaur Cove East in Victoria, Australia.
It is a basal iguanodontian ornithopod, a type of herbivorous dinosaur known for having a beak and complex dental batteries for grinding plants.
This small dinosaur was estimated to be about two to three meters long and weighed approximately 125 kilograms.
It lived during the Early Cretaceous period on the continent of Australia.
It is a rare Australian dinosaur whose name honors the Atlas Copco company that provided equipment for its discovery.

name: Atrociraptor
hints: Holotype fossils were found in the Horsethief Member of the Horseshoe Canyon Formation in Alberta, dating to the Maastrichtian age of approximately 72.2 to 71.5 million years ago.
It possessed a uniquely deep snout with teeth that were strongly inclined backwards and were almost all the same size, differing from most other dromaeosaurids.
This dinosaur was relatively small, measuring about 1.8 to 2 meters in length and weighing around 15 kilograms, and it was a carnivore.
It lived during the Late Cretaceous period in what is now North America, specifically in the region of modern-day Canada.
It is a feathered dromaeosaurid known for having a large, sickle-shaped claw on its second toe, similar to its famous relative Velociraptor.

name: Atsinganosaurus
hints: Fossils were excavated from the Grès à Reptiles Formation in the Aix-en-Provence Basin, specifically at the Velaux-La Bastide Neuve site.
It is classified as a titanosaurian sauropod, a group of long-necked herbivorous dinosaurs known for their pillar-like limbs.
Adults were relatively small for sauropods, measuring approximately 5 to 9 meters in length and weighing between 2.5 and 5 metric tons.
This dinosaur lived in what is now France during the Late Cretaceous period.
It is a type of giant, four-legged plant-eater with a very long neck and tail.

name: Aucasaurus
hints: Fossils were discovered in the Anacleto Formation of Argentina, dating to the Late Cretaceous period (Santonian to Campanian stages).
It possessed a pair of low ridges above each eye instead of the prominent horns seen in its relative Carnotaurus, and had extremely reduced arms with almost no fingers.
This medium-sized theropod measured approximately 5.5 to 6.2 meters in length and weighed around 700 kilograms, feeding on other animals.
It lived in South America during the Late Cretaceous epoch, sharing its habitat with numerous sauropods and other dinosaurs.
It is known for having some of the most reduced forelimbs of any dinosaur, with hands that lacked functional claws on most digits.

name: Augustynolophus
hints: Fossils were discovered in the Moreno Formation in California, dating to the late Maastrichtian age approximately 66 million years ago.
It possesses a distinctive hollow crest on its skull, a feature it shares with its close relative Saurolophus.
This large herbivore reached approximately 8 meters (26 feet) in length and weighed around 3 metric tons.
It lived in Laramidia, which corresponds to the western part of North America, during the very end of the Cretaceous period.
It was officially declared the state dinosaur of California in 2017.

name: Auroraceratops
hints: Fossils were recovered from the Zhonggou Formation in the Yujingzi Basin of Gansu Province, dating to the Early Cretaceous (Aptian-Albian), approximately 113 to 100 million years ago.
It possessed a short, wide, and flat skull featuring paired rugose (rough-textured) bony areas in front of the eyes and on the cheekbones, likely covered in keratin.
This bipedal herbivore was moderate-sized for its group, measuring about 1.25 meters (4.1 feet) in length and weighing roughly 15.5 kilograms (34 pounds).
It lived in what is now north-central China during the Early Cretaceous period.
It is one of the earliest known members of the horned dinosaur lineage (neoceratopsians), representing a 'dawn' stage in the evolution of later famous horned dinosaurs.

name: Aurornis
hints: Fossil evidence from the Tiaojishan Formation in western Liaoning, China, dated to the Oxfordian stage of the late Jurassic period, approximately 160 million years ago.
It possessed clawed wings and a long bony tail, with leg bones similar to Archaeopteryx but overall more primitive anatomy.
It was roughly the size of a modern pheasant, measuring about 50 cm (20 inches) in length.
It lived during the Jurassic period in what is now Asia, specifically China.
It is often cited as the most basal or primitive known member of the bird lineage, predating Archaeopteryx by about 10 million years.

name: Australodocus
hints: Fossils were recovered from the Upper Dinosaur Member of the Tendaguru Formation in Tanzania, dating to approximately 150 million years ago.
It possessed distinctive double (bifurcate) neural spines on its vertebrae, a feature often associated with diplodocoid sauropods.
This large herbivore is estimated to have reached lengths of about 17 meters and weighed roughly 4,000 kilograms.
It lived during the Late Jurassic period on the southern supercontinent of Gondwana, in what is now East Africa.
It is a massive, long-necked plant-eating dinosaur known as a sauropod.

name: Australovenator
hints: Fossils were discovered in the Late Cretaceous Winton Formation of Australia, dating back approximately 95 million years.
It possessed unusually flexible arms with a sliding radius and ulna, allowing it to grasp prey towards its chest.
This lightweight bipedal carnivore measured about 6 meters (20 feet) in length and weighed roughly 310 to 500 kilograms.
It lived in Australia during the Late Cretaceous period.
It is known as the 'cheetah of its time' due to its lightweight build and estimated running speed of 30 mph.

name: Austrocheirus
hints: Fossils were discovered in the Pari Aike Formation, dating to the Campanian to Maastrichtian stages of the Late Cretaceous.
It is a theropod dinosaur notable for having normal-sized forelimbs, unlike many of its close relatives which had reduced arms.
This medium-sized carnivore measured approximately 5.5 to 6.5 meters in length.
It lived in South America during the Late Cretaceous period.
Its name literally translates to 'southern hand'.

name: Austroposeidon
hints: Found in the Campanian-Maastrichtian Presidente Prudente Formation of Brazil, with the original fossil site lost to urban development after being discovered in 1953.
Possesses unique vertebral autapomorphies including columnar centropostzygapophyseal laminae and rear centrodiapophyseal laminae that split into two prongs, along with camellate bone texture.
An adult titanosaurian sauropod measuring approximately 25 meters (82 feet) in length, making it one of the largest dinosaurs ever discovered in Brazil.
Lived during the Late Cretaceous period on the continent of South America.
It is a massive, long-necked plant-eating dinosaur known as the 'Magnificent Southern Poseidon.'

name: Austroraptor
hints: Fossils were discovered in the Allen Formation of Río Negro, Argentina, dating to the Late Cretaceous period (Campanian to Maastrichtian ages).
It possessed unusually short forelimbs relative to its body size, with a humerus less than half the length of its femur, and conical, non-serrated teeth.
This large theropod measured approximately 5 to 6 meters in length and weighed between 300 and 520 kilograms, functioning as a carnivore.
It lived in South America during the Late Cretaceous period, making it one of the largest dromaeosaurids found in the Southern Hemisphere.
It is often compared to Tyrannosaurus rex due to its disproportionately short arms, despite being a member of the raptor family.

name: Austrosaurus
hints: Fossils were found in the Allaru Formation in Central-Western Queensland, Australia, dating to the early Cretaceous period (112-100 million years ago).
It possessed a distinctive nearly level back due to its shoulder and hip heights being almost identical at approximately 4 meters.
This sauropod reached an estimated length of 20 meters and weighed around 16 metric tons, feeding on vegetation.
It lived on dry land in Australia during the early Cretaceous period.
It is a large, long-necked plant-eating dinosaur from Australia, often confused with or closely related to Wintonotitan.

name: Avaceratops
hints: Fossils are primarily found in the Judith River Formation, dating to the late Campanian stage approximately 76 to 74 million years ago.
It possesses a distinctive solid frill with a large squamosal bone that has a continuously curving edge rather than a stepped one.
It was a small, herbivorous quadruped, with estimates suggesting a length of around 2.3 to 4.2 meters and a weight of roughly one tonne.
This dinosaur lived in what is now the northwestern United States during the Late Cretaceous period.
It is a horned dinosaur with a parrot-like beak, known for its relatively small size and solid bony frill compared to its larger relatives.

name: Aviatyrannis
hints: Fossils were discovered in the Alcobaça Formation of Portugal, dating to approximately 155 million years ago during the Late Jurassic.
The known remains include an elongated, low ilium featuring a vertical ridge on the outer blade, along with D-shaped premaxillary teeth and maxillary teeth with perpendicular denticles.
It was a small theropod, estimated to be about 1 meter long and weighing around 4 kilograms, likely feeding on small prey.
This dinosaur lived in what is now Europe during the Late Jurassic period.
It is considered one of the oldest known members of the tyrannosauroid lineage, representing an early stage in the evolution of tyrant dinosaurs.

name: Avimimus
hints: Fossils are found in the Nemegt Formation of Mongolia, dating to the Maastrichtian age of the Late Cretaceous, approximately 72 to 66 million years ago.
It possessed extremely long, slender legs with shins longer than its thighs, fused hand bones similar to modern birds, and a long, slender neck with elongated vertebrae.
This small dinosaur measured about 1.5 meters (5 feet) in length and likely weighed only a few kilograms, with a diet that was probably herbivorous or omnivorous.
It lived in what is now Mongolia during the Late Cretaceous period, sharing its floodplain habitat with large predators like Tarbosaurus.
It is widely recognized for its strikingly bird-like appearance, featuring a toothless beak, a large brain, and feathers, leading to its name meaning 'bird mimic'.

name: Baalsaurus
hints: Fossils were recovered from the upper Portezuelo Formation in Neuquén Province, Argentina, dating to the Late Cretaceous period.
It possessed a distinctive squared-off jawbone with teeth crowded tightly at the front, resembling the dentition of Antarctosaurus.
This was a massive, plant-eating titanosaurian sauropod dinosaur that lived during the age of dinosaurs.
It roamed the region of Patagonia in South America during the Late Cretaceous epoch.
It is a giant, long-necked herbivorous dinosaur known for its enormous size and tail.

name: Bactrosaurus
hints: Fossils were recovered from the Iren Dabasu Formation in the Gobi Desert, dating to approximately 95.8 million years ago.
It possessed distinctive large, club-shaped neural spines projecting from its vertebrae, which inspired its name meaning 'club lizard'.
This herbivore measured about 6 to 6.5 meters long and weighed roughly 1.2 metric tons, featuring an unusually powerful build for its group.
It lived in Asia during the Late Cretaceous period, representing one of the earliest known hadrosauroids.
It is a crestless, early relative of later duck-billed dinosaurs, often recognized for its robust, iguanodont-like appearance and lack of a head crest.

name: Bagaceratops
hints: Fossils are primarily found in the Barun Goyot Formation and Bayan Mandahu Formation, dating to approximately 72 to 71 million years ago.
It possessed a small frill and lacked the premaxillary teeth found in its close relative Protoceratops.
This dinosaur was quite small for a horned dinosaur, measuring only 1 to 1.5 meters long and weighing between 22 and 45 kilograms, and it was a plant-eater.
It lived in Asia during the Late Cretaceous period.
It is a small, beaked, horned dinosaur often associated with the famous Protoceratops in popular culture and media like Jurassic Park.

name: Bagaraatan
hints: Fossils were discovered in the Nemegt Formation of Mongolia, dating to the Late Cretaceous period approximately 70 million years ago.
It is classified as a tyrannosauroid, sharing close evolutionary ties with the famous T-rex family, though its exact placement within that group remains debated.
This dinosaur was relatively small for its lineage, measuring only about 3 to 4 meters (10 to 13 feet) in length, likely representing a juvenile specimen.
It lived during the Late Cretaceous period on the continent of Asia, specifically in what is now Mongolia.
It is a small, meat-eating relative of the Tyrannosaurus rex, belonging to the same major group of large predatory dinosaurs.

name: Bagualia
hints: Fossils were found in the Cañadón Asfalto Formation in Chubut Province, Argentina, dating to approximately 179 million years ago.
It possesses uniquely thick tooth enamel, roughly seven times thicker than other herbivores, and a distinctive beak-like process on the premaxilla.
This Early Jurassic eusauropod was a large, quadrupedal herbivore that likely grew to significant sizes to adapt to a harsh, volcanic climate.
It lived in South America during the Early Jurassic period, making it the oldest known definitive member of the eusauropod group.
It is recognized as a pivotal, ancient ancestor in the evolutionary lineage of giant, long-necked sauropod dinosaurs.

name: Bagualosaurus
hints: Fossils were discovered in the Candelária Sequence of the Uppermost Santa Maria Formation in Rio Grande do Sul, Brazil, dating to approximately 230 million years ago during the Carnian stage of the Late Triassic.
It possessed robust hindlimbs, a feature so distinctive that its genus name is derived from the local dialect word for a 'strongly built fellow' or sturdy horse.
This basal sauropodomorph was a modest-sized herbivore, measuring approximately 2.25 meters (7.4 feet) in length.
It lived in what is now South America during the Late Triassic period, specifically in the Carnian age.
It is a small, early relative of the giant long-necked dinosaurs, representing a transitional form between earlier and later sauropodomorphs.

name: Bahariasaurus
hints: Fossils were discovered in the Bahariya Formation of Egypt, dating to approximately 95 million years ago during the Cenomanian stage.
It is known only from postcranial bones, including distinctive vertebrae with longitudinally elongate pleurocoels and a pubis, but no skull material has been confidently assigned to it.
This giant theropod reached lengths of 11 to 12 meters and weighed around 4 tonnes, making it comparable in size to Tyrannosaurus rex.
It lived in North Africa during the Late Cretaceous period.
Its original type specimens were destroyed in a World War II bombing raid on Munich, leaving only illustrations as evidence of its existence.

name: Baiyinosaurus
hints: Fossils were discovered in the Straw-yellow Sandstone Member of the Wangjiashan Formation in the Pingchuan District of Baiyin, Gansu Province, China.
It possessed a unique skull roof structure where the paired frontal bones were wider than they were long, a trait that distinguishes it from most other stegosaurs.
This quadrupedal herbivore was a stegosaurian dinosaur, likely featuring a row of plates and spines along its back for defense and display.
It lived during the Middle Jurassic period on the continent of Asia.
It is easily recognized as a member of the stegosaur group, famous for the large bony plates and spikes lining its back.

name: Bajadasaurus
hints: Fossils were discovered in the Bajada Colorada Formation of the Neuquén Basin in northern Patagonia, Argentina, dating to the Early Cretaceous period between 145 and 132.9 million years ago.
It possessed extremely elongated, bifurcated (two-pronged) neural spines on its neck vertebrae that curved forward, a unique feature among its relatives.
This herbivorous sauropod was relatively small for its group, reaching a size comparable to a modern Asian elephant, with a short neck and a slender skull containing pencil-shaped teeth.
It lived during the Early Cretaceous epoch on the continent of South America, specifically in what is now Argentina.
Its most recognizable feature is the pair of long, forked spines sticking up from its neck, which likely served as a defensive structure against predators.

name: Bambiraptor
hints: The holotype fossil was unearthed in the Upper Two Medicine Formation of Montana, dating to the late Campanian stage of the Late Cretaceous, approximately 72 million years ago.
It possessed a highly flexible forelimb with mutually opposable first and third fingers, allowing it to bring its hands to its mouth to eat, a trait similar to some modern small mammals.
This was a very small, lightweight theropod, with adults estimated to be only about 1.3 meters long and weighing roughly 5 kilograms, and it was a carnivore.
It lived in what is now North America during the Late Cretaceous period.
Its genus name translates to 'Bambi thief,' referencing the young age of the first specimen found and the popular Disney character.

name: Banji
hints: Fossils were found in the Nanxiong Formation near Ganzhou, China, dating to the Cretaceous–Paleogene boundary approximately 66 million years ago.
It possessed a tall skull crest formed by the nasal bones and premaxillae, uniquely decorated with vertical striations and grooves on the lower jaw.
This small, bipedal dinosaur was estimated to be only 65 cm (2.1 ft) long and was a carnivore.
It lived in Asia during the late Cretaceous Period.
It is known for its distinctive, tall crest on its head that resembles a striped dragon.

name: Bannykus
hints: Fossils were found in the Early Cretaceous Bayin-Gobi Formation of Inner Mongolia, China.
It exhibits transitional forelimb features that are more reduced than early relatives but less specialized than later alvarezsaurs.
This subadult individual measured approximately 2.77 meters in length and weighed between 24 and 30 kilograms, with evidence suggesting a carnivorous diet.
It lived during the Early Cretaceous period on the continent of Asia.
It is a theropod dinosaur known for its unique, partially reduced arms that bridge the evolutionary gap between typical clawed predators and later insect-eating specialists.

name: Baotianmansaurus
hints: Fossils were discovered in the Gaogou Formation of Henan Province, China, dating to the Late Cretaceous period.
It is distinguished by unique vertebral features, including short neural spines on mid-back vertebrae and a developed system of pneumatic openings.
This medium-sized sauropod measured approximately 20 to 22.5 meters in length and weighed between 15 and 21 metric tons, feeding on plants.
It lived in Asia during the Late Cretaceous epoch.
It is a massive, long-necked plant-eating dinosaur with a tail and four sturdy legs.

name: Barapasaurus
hints: Fossils were discovered in the Lower Kota Formation, dating to approximately 184–200 million years ago, near the village of Pochampally in central India.
It possessed a long neck and columnar limbs, with teeth that were spoon-shaped, showed wrinkled enamel, and featured coarse serrations.
This early sauropod measured about 12 to 14 meters in length and weighed roughly 7 tonnes, feeding on vegetation.
It lived during the Early to Middle Jurassic period on the landmass that is now the Indian subcontinent.
It is one of the most completely known early sauropods, with over 300 bones found from at least six individuals, yet its skull remains largely missing.

name: Barilium
hints: Fossils were discovered in the Wadhurst Clay formation in East Sussex, England, dating to the middle Valanginian stage of the Early Cretaceous period.
It possessed a robust build with large vertebrae characterized by short neural spines, distinguishing it from related iguanodontians with long, narrow spines.
This herbivorous dinosaur was estimated to reach approximately 8 meters (26 feet) in length.
It lived in what is now England during the Early Cretaceous period.
It is a type of iguanodontian dinosaur, closely related to the well-known Iguanodon.

name: Barosaurus
hints: Fossils were discovered in the Morrison Formation, dating to the Late Jurassic period, specifically in states like South Dakota, Utah, and Montana.
It possessed a unique vertebral count with only nine dorsal vertebrae and potentially sixteen neck vertebrae, along with forked chevron bones in the tail featuring prominent forward spikes.
This giant herbivore reached lengths of up to 40 meters and weighed around 60 tons, featuring a disproportionately long neck and a shorter tail compared to its relative Diplodocus.
It lived in what is now North America during the Late Jurassic epoch, sharing its habitat with other famous sauropods.
It is famous for having one of the longest necks of any known dinosaur, allowing it to browse vegetation at great heights.

name: Barrosasaurus
hints: Fossils were discovered in the Anacleto Formation of Neuquén province, Argentina, dating to the Late Cretaceous period.
It possessed large, bulbous dorsal vertebrae with internal chambers, a common trait among titanosaur sauropods.
This massive herbivore reached approximately 18 meters in length and weighed around 13.5 tonnes.
It lived in South America during the Late Cretaceous period, roughly 80 to 70 million years ago.
It was a giant, long-necked plant-eating dinosaur with a massive, barrel-shaped body.

name: Barsboldia
hints: Fossils were discovered in the Maastrichtian-aged Nemegt Formation in Mongolia.
It possessed extremely tall neural spines over the hips, with the tips of some tail vertebrae being club-shaped.
This large herbivore likely reached lengths of 12 to 14 meters and weighed around 5 metric tons.
It lived in Asia during the Late Cretaceous period.
It is a duck-billed dinosaur known for its distinctive, unusually tall back spines.

name: Baryonyx
hints: Holotype specimen found in the Weald Clay Formation, Surrey, England, dating to the Barremian stage of the Early Cretaceous (approx. 130–125 million years ago).
Possesses a distinctive elongated, crocodile-like snout and a massive, sickle-shaped claw on the first finger of its forelimb.
An approximately 7.5 to 10-meter long theropod that weighed around 1.2 to 2 metric tons and was one of the first dinosaurs proven to eat fish.
Lived in what is now Europe during the Early Cretaceous period.
Famous for its giant, curved claw on its hand and its fish-eating habits.

name: Bashanosaurus
hints: Fossils were recovered from the Shaximiao Formation in Yunyang County, China, dating to the Bathonian age of the Middle Jurassic, approximately 166 million years ago.
It possesses a small, triangular acromial process on the scapula and a fourth trochanter on the femur positioned below the midpoint of the shaft, distinguishing it from later stegosaurs.
This quadrupedal herbivore was equipped with a row of plates and spines along its back and tail for defense, representing one of the earliest and most basal members of the Stegosauria clade.
It lived in what is now Asia during the Middle Jurassic period, making it the oldest named stegosaur known from that continent.
It is a stegosaur, the dinosaur famous for the large bony plates running down its back and the spikes on its tail.

name: Bashunosaurus
hints: Fossils were found in the Middle Jurassic Shaximiao Formation in Kaijiang, China, dating back approximately 164 to 161 million years.
The known skeleton includes six cervical vertebrae, eight dorsal vertebrae, and parts of the limbs and shoulder girdle, indicating a robust build.
It was a large quadrupedal herbivore, estimated to be between 10 and 12 meters long.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Middle Jurassic period.
It is a long-necked, four-legged plant-eating dinosaur, commonly recognized as a sauropod.

name: Batyrosaurus
hints: Found in the Bostobe Formation of central Kazakhstan, dating to the Santonian-Campanian stages of the Late Cretaceous, approximately 84 million years ago.
Possesses unique skull features including elongated frontal bones that are about 70% longer than wide, and parietal bones at the rear that form bony flaps overlapping the supraoccipital.
A medium-sized herbivore measuring approximately five to six meters in length, identified as a basal hadrosauroid dinosaur.
Lived during the Late Cretaceous period on the continent of Asia.
It is a type of duck-billed dinosaur, known for its specialized beak and herbivorous diet.

name: Baurutitan
hints: Fossils were recovered from the Serra da Galga Formation in Minas Gerais, Brazil, dating to the Maastrichtian stage approximately 72 to 66 million years ago.
It is distinguished from related titanosaurs by its unique and distinctive caudal vertebrae.
This sauropod was a large herbivore estimated to reach lengths of 12 to 14 meters and stand about 3.5 meters tall.
It lived in South America during the Late Cretaceous period.
It is a titanosaur, a type of long-necked, plant-eating dinosaur with a massive body and tail.

name: Bayannurosaurus
hints: Found in the Bayin-Gobi Formation of China, dating to the early Aptian stage of the Early Cretaceous period
It was a large iguanodontian ornithopod, classified as an intermediate form within the Ankylopollexia clade
This herbivore reached a total body length of approximately 9 meters (30 feet)
It lived in what is now Asia during the Early Cretaceous epoch
It is a well-preserved, nearly complete skeleton of a large, beaked plant-eating dinosaur from the age of dinosaurs

name: Beibeilong
hints: Fossils were found in the Gaogou Formation of Henan Province, dating to the Late Cretaceous, approximately 96 to 88 million years ago.
It possessed a toothless skull with a horny beak and a unique deep depression on the lacrimal bone that allowed the frontal bone to overlap.
This large oviraptorosaur reached an estimated length of 7.5 meters and weighed between 1.5 and 2 metric tons, likely feeding on plants or small animals.
It lived in what is now China during the Late Cretaceous period, inhabiting humid floodplains with semi-arid to tropical climates.
It is famous for being the dinosaur associated with the 'Baby Louie' embryo, which was featured on the cover of National Geographic Magazine.

name: Beipiaosaurus
hints: Fossils were recovered from the Yixian Formation in Liaoning Province, China, dating to the Aptian stage of the Early Cretaceous, approximately 125 million years ago.
It possessed a distinctive three-toed foot structure, differing from the four-toed feet of more advanced therizinosaurs, and had a notably shorter neck relative to its body size.
This dinosaur was a small theropod, measuring about 2.2 meters (7.2 feet) in length and weighing approximately 27 to 50 kilograms, and it was a herbivore.
It lived in what is now China during the Early Cretaceous period.
It is famous for being one of the heaviest dinosaurs known from direct fossil evidence to be covered in feathers, with preserved skin impressions showing brownish plumage.

name: Beishanlong
hints: Fossils were discovered in the Xiagou Formation of the Xinminpu Group, dating to the late Aptian stage of the Early Cretaceous, specifically at the White Ghost Castle site in Gansu, China.
It possessed a robust build with long arms and legs, but notably lacked the extremely elongated hands, feet, and claws seen in later, more derived ornithomimosaurs.
This giant ornithomimosaur reached lengths of approximately 5.9 to 8 meters and weighed between 375 and 626 kilograms, making it one of the largest members of its group.
It lived during the Early Cretaceous period in what is now Asia, specifically in the northwestern region of China.
It is visually recognized as a large, ostrich-like dinosaur with a long neck and tail, often compared to its relative Deinocheirus due to its massive size.

name: Bellusaurus
hints: Fossils were discovered in the Shishugou Formation, dating to the Oxfordian stage of the Late Jurassic, specifically within the northeastern Junggar Basin of China.
This sauropod is distinguished by its broad, spatulate teeth and a lightly built, slender skeletal structure compared to other giant long-necked dinosaurs.
Based on juvenile specimens, this dinosaur reached an estimated length of 4.8 meters (16 feet) and was a herbivore.
It lived in what is now China during the Late Jurassic period, approximately 163 to 157 million years ago.
It is notable for being found as a mass death assemblage of seventeen individuals in a single quarry, suggesting a herd killed by a flash flood.

name: Berberosaurus
hints: Fossils were recovered from the Azilal Formation in the Central High Atlas of Morocco, dating to the Toarcian age of the Early Jurassic, approximately 183 million years ago.
It is characterized by highly pneumatic cervical vertebrae with short centra and holes in the neural arch, along with a metacarpal featuring a very grooved proximal end.
This bipedal carnivore measured approximately 4.58 to 5 meters in length and weighed around 300 kilograms.
It lived in North Africa during the Early Jurassic period, making it one of the few formally identified theropods from that region and time.
It is considered one of the oldest known ceratosaur dinosaurs, representing a basal member of this group that diversified earlier than previously thought.

name: Berthasaura
hints: Fossils were discovered at the Cemitério dos Pterossauros site within the Goio-Erê Formation of Paraná, Brazil.
It possessed a unique toothless, short beak and showed convergent adaptations with oviraptorids, having lost its teeth as it matured.
This dinosaur was small, measuring approximately 1 metre (3.3 ft) in length, and was likely a herbivore or omnivore.
It lived in what is now South America during the Early Cretaceous period, specifically in an ancient desert environment.
It is one of the few known theropod dinosaurs that was herbivorous or omnivorous rather than strictly carnivorous.

name: Bicentenaria
hints: Fossils were discovered in the Candeleros Formation of Argentina, dating to the Cenomanian stage of the early Late Cretaceous, approximately 99 to 94 million years ago.
It possessed a distinctive quadratojugal bone with an anterior branch that is twice as long as its ascending branch, a feature that sets it apart from many close relatives.
This was a small, bipedal carnivore measuring between 2.5 and 3 meters (8 to 10 feet) in length, weighing only a few dozen kilograms.
It lived in what is now South America during the Late Cretaceous period, sharing its environment with giant titanosaur sauropods.
It is named for the bicentennial (200th anniversary) of Argentina's May Revolution, reflecting its discovery in that country.

name: Bissektipelta
hints: Fossils were discovered in the Bissekty Formation of Uzbekistan, dating to the Late Cretaceous period (approximately 90 million years ago).
It possessed a distinctive skull roof with grooves forming a truncated 'Y' shape that separated three polygonal areas of flat bone, reflecting the position of its armor plates.

This was a heavily armored, quadrupedal herbivore with a low-slung body, likely weighing several tons, and recent studies suggest it may have had an omnivorous diet including filter-feeding capabilities.
It lived in Asia during the Late Cretaceous epoch, a time when dinosaurs dominated the terrestrial ecosystems of the supercontinent Laurasia.
It is a type of ankylosaur, instantly recognizable as a 'tank-like' dinosaur covered in bony armor plates and osteoderms, often featuring a clubbed tail.

name: Bistahieversor
hints: Fossils were recovered from the Hunter Wash Member of the Kirtland Formation and the Fossil Forest member of the Fruitland Formation in New Mexico, dating to approximately 75.5 to 74.5 million years ago.
It possessed 64 teeth, a keel along the lower jaw, and a unique extra opening above the eye that likely housed an air sac to lighten the skull.
Adults were large predators measuring around 9 meters (30 feet) in length and weighing between 2.5 and 3.3 metric tons, feeding on other animals.
This dinosaur lived during the Late Cretaceous period in what is now North America, specifically in the region of present-day New Mexico.
It is often nicknamed the 'Bisti Beast' due to the Bisti/De-Na-Zin Wilderness Area where many of its specimens were discovered.

name: Bisticeratops
hints: Found in the Kirtland Formation (Farmington Member) of the Bisti/De-Na-Zin Wilderness in New Mexico, dating to the late Campanian age of the late Cretaceous period
A chasmosaurine ceratopsian known from a nearly complete skull, originally misidentified as Pentacerops despite being two million years younger
Approximately the size of other large horned dinosaurs, this herbivore bore bite marks from tyrannosaurids that show signs of healing
Lived on the coastal plain of western North America during the late Cretaceous period
A horned dinosaur with a large frill and facial horns, whose name literally translates to 'horned face from Bisti'

name: Blasisaurus
hints: Found in the upper Maastrichtian layer of the Arén Formation in Spain, dating to approximately 66 million years ago
Possesses a distinctive cheekbone with a rear projection featuring a hook-shaped upper edge and a narrow, D-shaped lower temporal fenestra
A medium-sized ornithopod herbivore that lived during the Late Cretaceous period
Lived in Europe, specifically in what is now Spain, during the final stage of the Mesozoic Era
It is a crested duck-billed dinosaur, part of the lambeosaurine group known for their hollow head crests

name: Bolong
hints: Fossils were discovered in the Early Cretaceous Yixian Formation of western Liaoning Province, China, dating to approximately 125 million years ago.
It possesses a distinctive vertical ledge dividing the inside of its maxillary teeth, along with a unique cavity at the interface of the lacrimal and maxilla bones.
This was a relatively small herbivore, estimated to reach 4 meters in length and weigh around 200 kilograms.
It lived during the Early Cretaceous period on the continent of Asia.
It is considered one of the most primitive members of the group that would later evolve into the famous duck-billed dinosaurs.

name: Bonapartenykus
hints: Fossils were discovered in the Allen Formation of Río Negro Province, Argentina, dating to the Late Cretaceous (Campanian–Maastrichtian stages).
It is distinguished by unique spinal features, specifically spinopostzygapophyseal laminae that end abruptly above the postzygapophyses, and a scapula with a very wide notch on its posterior margin.
This dinosaur measured approximately 2.5 to 3.3 meters in length and weighed between 43 and 72 kilograms, making it the largest known member of the alvarezsauroid clade.
It lived in South America during the Late Cretaceous period, sharing its habitat with titanosaur sauropods, hadrosaurids, and abelisaurid theropods.
It is notable for being found in close association with two incomplete eggs and several clusters of eggshells, which were distinct enough to be named a new oogenus.

name: Bonapartesaurus
hints: Fossils were excavated from the lower member of the Allen Formation in the Río Negro province of Argentina, dating to the Campanian to Maastrichtian stages of the Late Cretaceous.
It is distinguished by extremely long neural spines on its anterior tail vertebrae, which are three to four times the height of the centra, and a specific asymmetry in its ilium structure.
This herbivorous hadrosaurid was a large ornithopod, likely reaching lengths of several meters, that grazed on the abundant Late Cretaceous flora of South America.
It lived in what is now South America (specifically Argentina) during the Late Cretaceous period, roughly 83 to 66 million years ago.

It is a member of the duck-billed dinosaur family (Hadrosauridae), recognizable by its broad, flat snout adapted for shearing vegetation, though it lacked the elaborate head crests seen in some relatives.

name: Bonatitan
hints: Late Cretaceous Allen Formation, Argentina, approximately 83.6 to 70.6 million years ago
Titanosaurian sauropod with a distinctive braincase and caudal vertebrae
Large herbivorous quadruped, likely reaching lengths of 15 to 20 meters
South America during the Late Cretaceous period
A massive, long-necked plant-eating dinosaur

name: Bonitasaura
hints: Found in the uppermost Santonian stage of the Bajo de la Carpa Formation in the Neuquén Basin of Río Negro Province, Argentina
Possessed a distinctive sharp ridge on the lower jaw that supported a sharp, beak-like keratin sheath, along with reversed limb proportions where the front limbs were shorter than the hind limbs
Measured approximately 10 metres (33 ft) in length and was a herbivore that used its beak-like structure to crop vegetation
Lived during the Late Cretaceous period in what is now South America
It is a titanosaurian sauropod known for having a skull and jaw structure that closely resembles those of diplodocids rather than typical long-necked giants

name: Borealopelta
hints: Fossils were found in the Wabiskaw Member of the Clearwater Formation in Alberta, dating to approximately 112 million years ago during the Albian stage.
It possessed a straight tail without a club, but featured distinctive long, bull-like horns protruding from its shoulders and rows of armor plates.
This herbivore measured about 5.5 meters in length and weighed roughly 1.3 metric tons, feeding primarily on ferns.
It lived in what is now North America during the Early Cretaceous period, when the region was covered by the Western Interior Seaway.
It is famous for being an exceptionally preserved 'mummy' that retained original skin, soft tissue, and reddish-brown camouflage coloration.

name: Borealosaurus
hints: Fossils were discovered in the Sunjiawan Formation of Liaoning, northern China, dating to the Late Cretaceous period.
It possessed a distinctive vertebral structure lacking procoely in the middle caudal vertebrae, distinguishing it from more derived titanosaur groups.
This was a massive, long-necked herbivorous sauropod dinosaur that likely weighed several tons and used its bulk to process vegetation.
It lived during the Late Cretaceous epoch on the continent of Asia, specifically in what is now northeastern China.
It is a giant, four-legged plant-eater with an extremely long neck and tail, resembling a massive land-based marine reptile.

name: Boreonykus
hints: Fossil remains were found in the Wapiti Formation of central Alberta, dating to approximately 73.27 million years ago.
It possessed a distinctive sickle-shaped claw on its foot, a hallmark trait of its family.
It was a small, carnivorous predator, likely measuring around one meter in length.
It lived during the Late Cretaceous period on the continent of North America.
It is a feathered, bird-like dinosaur known for its sharp, curved claws.

name: Borogovia
hints: Fossils were discovered in the Nemegt Formation, dating to the early Maastrichtian stage of the Late Cretaceous, specifically at the Altan Uul IV site in Mongolia.
It is distinguished by the absence of the characteristic hyperextendable 'sickle claw' on the second toe, a feature common in many other troodontids and dromaeosaurids.
This small theropod measured approximately two meters in length and weighed around twenty kilograms, with notably elongated lower leg bones.
It lived in what is now Mongolia during the Late Cretaceous period, part of the broader Mesozoic Era.
Its genus name is derived from the 'borogoves,' a whimsical creature described in Lewis Carroll's poem 'Jabberwocky'.

name: Brachiosaurus
hints: Fossils were discovered in the Brushy Basin Member of the Morrison Formation, dating to the Late Jurassic approximately 154 to 153 million years ago.
It possessed disproportionately long forelimbs compared to its hindlimbs, creating a steeply inclined trunk and a giraffe-like posture.
This massive herbivore reached lengths of 18 to 22 meters and weighed up to 47 metric tons, allowing it to browse vegetation as high as 9 meters off the ground.
It lived in North America during the Late Jurassic period, roughly 155 to 145 million years ago.
It is widely recognized as the iconic dinosaur from the 1993 film Jurassic Park, famous for its towering height and long neck.

name: Brachylophosaurus
hints: Fossils have been recovered from the Oldman Formation in Alberta and the Judith River Formation in Montana, dating to the Late Cretaceous approximately 76 to 75 million years ago.
This dinosaur possessed a distinctive, blunt, and relatively short crest on its head formed by the nasal bones, which distinguishes it from other crested hadrosaurs.
It was a large herbivore, reaching lengths of up to 9 meters (30 feet) and weighing around 4 tons, utilizing a beak and dental batteries to process tough vegetation.
It lived in western North America during the Late Cretaceous period, sharing its environment with other famous dinosaurs like Tyrannosaurus and Triceratops.
It is one of the 'dinosaur mummies' famous for being discovered with extensive, well-preserved skin impressions and soft tissue details.

name: Brachytrachelopan
hints: Fossils were discovered in the Cañadón Calcáreo Formation of Chubut Province, Argentina, dating to the Late Jurassic (Oxfordian to Tithonian stages).
It possesses the shortest neck of any known sauropod, with individual cervical vertebrae being as long as or shorter than they are high.
This small sauropod measured approximately 10 to 11 meters in length and weighed around 5 metric tons, feeding on low-growing vegetation between 1 and 2 meters high.
It lived in South America during the Late Jurassic period, part of the supercontinent Gondwana.
It is best known for its uniquely short, giraffe-like neck, which is the shortest relative to body size of any dinosaur in the sauropod group.

name: Brasilotitan
hints: Fossils were discovered in the Adamantina Formation of Brazil, dating to the early Maastrichtian stage of the Late Cretaceous, approximately 72 million years ago.
It possessed a distinctive L-shaped lower jaw that created a squared-off snout, a feature shared with a specific clade of titanosaurs.
This sauropod was relatively small for its group, measuring less than 12 meters in length, and was a herbivore.
It lived in what is now South America during the Late Cretaceous period.
It is a titanosaur, a type of long-necked, plant-eating dinosaur known for its massive size, though this specific species was on the smaller side.

name: Bravasaurus
hints: Fossils were recovered from the Late Cretaceous Ciénaga del Río Huaco Formation in La Rioja, Argentina.
It possessed a robust skeleton with distinct vertebrae, including cervical, dorsal, and caudal types, along with limb bones like the femur and humerus.
This titanosaurian sauropod measured approximately 7 meters in length and weighed nearly 2.9 metric tons, feeding on vegetation.
It lived during the Late Cretaceous period on the continent of South America.
It is a massive, long-necked plant-eating dinosaur known as a titanosaur.

name: Bravoceratops
hints: Fossils were recovered from the lowermost rocks of the Javelina Formation in Big Bend National Park, Texas, dating to approximately 70 million years ago.
It possessed a distinctive hollow, tear-shaped depression on the median bar of its skull frill, which inspired its specific name referencing the single eye of the cyclops Polyphemus.
This large chasmosaurine ceratopsid had compressed, taller-than-wide brow horns and an epijugal horn that was longer than it was tall.
It lived during the Late Cretaceous period in what is now North America, specifically in the southwestern United States.
It is a horned dinosaur with a large bony frill, similar in general appearance to Triceratops but lacking the three iconic horns.

name: Breviceratops
hints: Fossils were discovered in the Barun Goyot Formation of the Nemegt Basin in Mongolia, dating to the Late Campanian stage of the Late Cretaceous.
It possessed a distinctive narrow, straight-shaped antorbital fenestra located in front of the eye socket, along with primitive premaxillary teeth.
This was a small, herbivorous dinosaur with a parrot-like beak, a moderately pronounced neck frill, and an estimated length of around 2 meters.
It lived in what is now Mongolia during the Late Cretaceous period, sharing its habitat with other famous dinosaurs like Velociraptor.
It is closely related to the famous egg-theft dinosaur Protoceratops and is often recognized for its small, frilled, beaked appearance.

name: Brighstoneus
hints: Fossils were recovered from the Wessex Formation on the Isle of Wight, dating to the Barremian stage approximately 125 million years ago.
It possessed a unique, S-shaped nasal bulla (a dorsal bulge above the nostril) and a heart-shaped predentary bone with large denticles.
It was a medium-sized herbivore, estimated to be about 6 meters long and weighing around 1 ton.
It lived in what is now England during the Early Cretaceous period.
It is a basal member of the group that eventually led to the duck-billed dinosaurs.

name: Brontomerus
hints: Fossils were recovered from the Ruby Ranch Member of the Cedar Mountain Formation in eastern Utah, dating to approximately 110 million years ago during the early Cretaceous.
It possessed a uniquely deep and oddly-shaped hipbone (ilium) that supported the largest thigh muscles, proportionally, of any known sauropod dinosaur.
The adult specimen is estimated to have weighed around six tonnes and measured approximately 14 meters in length, while being a plant-eating herbivore.
This long-necked dinosaur lived in what is now North America during the early Cretaceous period.
It is nicknamed the 'thunder thigh' dinosaur due to its exceptionally powerful leg muscles, which may have been used for kicking or traversing rough terrain.

name: Brontosaurus
hints: Fossils were discovered in the Morrison Formation, specifically at localities like Como Bluff in Wyoming and Garden Park in Colorado, dating to the Late Jurassic period approximately 156 to 146 million years ago.
This dinosaur possesses extremely robust and heavily built cervical vertebrae, giving it a thick, sturdy neck that distinguishes it from its more lightly built relatives like Diplodocus.
It was a massive herbivorous quadruped, with the largest species reaching lengths of 21 to 23 meters and weighing between 15 and 20 tons.
It lived in what is now the western United States during the Late Jurassic epoch, sharing its environment with Stegosaurus and Allosaurus.
It is widely recognized as the archetypal 'long-necked' dinosaur in popular culture and media, often serving as the visual standard for sauropods.
